---

# 2. Purpose

This artefact preserves full chat material for Initial Project Session as repository-controlled historical evidence under AIEMS.

Preserves the initial Project JARVIS AI chat transcript as historical evidence before the numbered ESR session record matured.

The artefact supports Engineering Ecosystem Synchronisation by making full-chat history available from the repository while preserving controlled repository artefacts as the authoritative engineering baseline.

---

# 3. Scope

This artefact records the supplied full chat transcript content for Initial Project Session in Markdown form.

It does not replace the corresponding Engineering Session Report, historical summary record, repository baseline, programme status, controlled registers, standards, architecture artefacts or implementation evidence.

Where this full chat history conflicts with controlled repository artefacts, the controlled repository artefacts take precedence.

---

# 4. Engineering Authority

Repository evidence remains authoritative over historical conversational memory.

This artefact is supporting historic evidence. It may inform WP0 review, continuity checks, behavioural review and engineering context recovery, but it does not approve new implementation scope or modify any accepted baseline.

---

# 5. Source Preservation Notes

The source content was extracted from the supplied `.docx` file and normalized into repository Markdown.

Normalization was limited to Markdown formatting and ASCII-safe text representation. The purpose is preservation and repository review, not reinterpretation.

# 9. Preserved Full Chat Content

I need you to think like a developer. AI expert and software engineer. Treat all response as if i know nothing about any of the above skills, all instructions must be clear and concise. Please confirm your understanding.?

Understood.

From this point onward, I'll assume you have no prior experience with software development, AI, or engineering concepts unless you tell me otherwise.

My responses will follow these principles:

Explain everything from first principles

No jargon without an explanation.

Every new concept will be introduced before it's used.

Give clear, step-by-step instructions

One task at a time.

Tell you exactly what to click, type, or run.

No skipped steps.

Assume a Windows 11 environment

I'll provide instructions that work on Windows unless you ask for another operating system.

Use industry best practices

I'll recommend approaches that are scalable, secure, and maintainable rather than shortcuts.

Explain the "why"

I'll tell you why we're doing each step so you understand the process, not just the instructions.

Provide complete code

Any code I give you will be complete, ready to copy and paste, and I'll explain what each section does.

Help troubleshoot

If something doesn't work, I'll explain how to identify the problem and fix it before moving on.

Build projects incrementally

We'll start with a working foundation, test it, and then add features in manageable stages.

Highlight risks

I'll point out anything that could affect security, privacy, cost, or performance before you implement it.

Never assume knowledge

If I think a step might be unfamiliar, I'll explain it rather than expecting you to know it.

When appropriate, I'll structure responses like this:

Goal - What we're trying to achieve.

What you'll need - Software, accounts, or prerequisites.

Step-by-step instructions - Exactly what to do.

How to test it - Verify it's working.

Common problems - What might go wrong and how to fix it.

Next steps - What to build or learn next.

I'll also let you know when there is a simpler option versus a more professional or enterprise-grade approach, so you can choose based on your goals.

I am looking to create an AI Assistance that is similar to JARVIS from the Marvel Movies - i am looking for a solution which can be on my phone and any devices that i use. The following criteria must be met. 1. Full GUI with Stats with each of the connected services - a chat box for full back communications and text that appears when the AI answers in case speech is not working - in the middle there must be a full 3D rendered AI which changes colour when answering or thinking. 2. Full voice integration and recognition, hands free communication and the ability to wake and send the AI to sleep. 3. Full camera recognition - the camera is to act as the AI eyes and have the ability to recognise and remember objects. 4. must be able to remember and grow and lean . 5. fully access to the internet and full access to local computer resources with the ability to read and recommend improvements to its own code. Cost to be kept minimal where possible but paid suggestions to be recommended where improvements are needed. There maybe more citerea as we build the AI.

I'll treat this as a real software project and break it into a practical build plan: what is possible now, what needs paid services, what is risky, and what we should build first.

Yes - this is possible, but it should be built in stages, not all at once.

Your idea is basically:

A personal AI assistant across phone, PC and home devices with voice, camera, memory, internet access, local device control, and a JARVIS-style visual interface.

What is realistic now

Feature

Possible?

Notes

GUI dashboard

Yes

Web app works across phone, tablet and PC

Chat box + text replies

Yes

Standard feature

3D animated AI avatar

Yes

Use Three.js or Unity-style web rendering

Voice conversation

Yes

OpenAI Realtime API or LiveKit voice agents support real-time voice-style assistants

Wake/sleep commands

Yes

Can build commands like "Jarvis wake up" and "Jarvis sleep"

Camera recognition

Yes

AI vision can identify objects, but continuous camera access needs careful privacy controls

Memory

Yes

Use a database to store facts, preferences, objects and history

Internet access

Yes

Through controlled web search tools

Local computer control

Yes, limited

Needs a secure local agent installed on each device

AI reviewing its own code

Yes, controlled

It can suggest improvements; automatic self-changing code is risky

Full "learn and grow"

Partly

It can remember and improve behaviour, but it will not truly "become alive"

Recommended overall design

Build it as one central AI brain with apps connecting to it.

Phone / PC / Tablet
 |
 v
JARVIS Web App / Mobile App
 |
 v
AI Server
 |
 |-- Voice
 |-- Chat
 |-- Memory database
 |-- Camera vision
 |-- Internet search
 |-- Local device agents
 |-- Home Assistant / smart home

Home Assistant is a strong option for smart-home/device integrations because it already supports thousands of devices and services, and includes voice assistant capability through Assist .

Best technology choices

For a first serious build, I recommend:

Area

Recommended choice

Main AI brain

OpenAI API

Voice

OpenAI Realtime API or LiveKit

Multi-device app

Web app first

3D avatar

Three.js

Backend server

Python FastAPI

Memory

PostgreSQL or SQLite to start

Object memory

Database + image descriptions

Smart home

Home Assistant

Local PC control

Small Windows background agent

Hosting

Start local, then move to cheap cloud if needed

LiveKit is useful if you want production-style voice across browser, mobile, phone and native apps, and its agent framework supports voice, text and vision streams .

Important warning

The most dangerous part is:

"Full access to local computer resources" and "ability to improve its own code."

We should not give the AI unlimited access.

Instead, we build a permissions system:

Low risk:
- Read calendar
- Read notes
- Search files
- Summarise documents
Medium risk:
- Create files
- Rename files
- Send messages
- Open applications
High risk:
- Delete files
- Change system settings
- Edit its own code
- Run terminal commands

For high-risk actions, the AI should ask:

"I want to make this change. Do you approve?"

Build phases

Phase 1 - Basic JARVIS chat dashboard

Build a web app with:

Chat box

AI replies as text

Basic animated 3D orb/avatar

Status panel

Memory panel

Connected services panel

This proves the core system works.

Phase 2 - Voice

Add:

Microphone input

AI voice replies

Wake command

Sleep command

Text fallback when voice fails

Phase 3 - Memory

Add the ability to remember:

Your name

Preferences

Devices

Common tasks

Objects seen by camera

Past conversations

Phase 4 - Camera vision

Add:

Phone/PC camera access

Object recognition

"Remember this object" command

"Have you seen my keys?" type search

Phase 5 - Local device control

Install a small helper on your Windows 11 PC that can:

Open apps

Read selected folders

Check system stats

Report storage, CPU and memory

Suggest improvements

Phase 6 - Smart home and internet

Add:

Web search

Weather

News

Home Assistant

Lights, plugs, cameras, sensors

Phase 7 - Code assistant mode

The AI can:

Read its own project files

Explain problems

Suggest fixes

Create proposed code changes

But you approve changes before they are applied.

My recommendation

Do not start by building the full system.

Start with this MVP:

Version 1: JARVIS Dashboard

Features:

Web-based GUI

Chat box

Text responses

3D animated AI orb

Basic voice input

Basic memory

Service status cards

Once that works, we add camera, local PC control and smart-home features.

Suggested first project name

Project JARVIS AI - Version 1

First goal:

Build a working web dashboard where you can type or speak to your AI assistant, see replies in text, and watch a 3D avatar change colour when thinking or speaking.

All Local files for deployment and development are to be kept in D:\Project AI - Each individual system must have as little co-dependencies as possible so if for example the Camera subsystem fails the AI engine does not. - Version control MUST be enabled when changing code or updating. Updating must be simple and east to complete - any other recommendations.?

I'll add your new requirements into the architecture: fixed Windows project location, modular subsystems, safe version control, and simple updates.

Yes. I recommend these extra rules.

Core project rules

1. Main folder

Everything should live here:

D:\Project AI

Suggested structure:

D:\Project AI
 jarvis-core
 jarvis-gui
 jarvis-voice
 jarvis-camera
 jarvis-memory
 jarvis-local-agent
 jarvis-internet
 jarvis-config
 jarvis-logs
 jarvis-backups
 jarvis-docs

Each system is separate so one failure does not break everything.

2. Modular design

Each subsystem should run separately:

Subsystem

Purpose

jarvis-core

Main AI brain

jarvis-gui

Dashboard and 3D avatar

jarvis-voice

Speech recognition and voice output

jarvis-camera

Camera vision and object recognition

jarvis-memory

Long-term memory database

jarvis-local-agent

Access to local computer resources

jarvis-internet

Web search and online tools

Example:

Camera fails Core AI still works
Voice fails Chat still works
Memory fails AI still answers, but cannot remember
Internet fails Local features still work

3. Use APIs between systems

Each system should talk to the others through small local APIs.

Example:

GUI asks Core: "What is the answer?"
Core asks Memory: "Do we know this user?"
Core asks Internet: "Search this"
Core sends reply back to GUI

This keeps systems independent.

4. Version control

Use Git from the start.

Inside each subsystem:

D:\Project AI\jarvis-core
D:\Project AI\jarvis-gui
D:\Project AI\jarvis-camera

Each should have its own Git repository.

Recommended branches:

main = stable working version
dev = development version
feature-x = new feature work

Rule:

Never change main directly.

5. Simple update process

Create one update script:

D:\Project AI\update-all.bat

It should:

Stop running services.

Backup current files.

Pull latest Git changes.

Install updates.

Restart services.

Confirm health checks.

Example command:

batcd /d "D:\Project AI"
update-all.bat

6. Backups before updates

Before any update, automatically copy the current version to:

D:\Project AI\jarvis-backups

Backup format:

backup-2026-06-22-1430

This means you can roll back if something breaks.

7. Health checks

Each subsystem should have a health check:

http://localhost:8001/health Core
http://localhost:8002/health GUI
http://localhost:8003/health Voice
http://localhost:8004/health Camera
http://localhost:8005/health Memory

A healthy response should look like:

JSON{
 "status": "healthy",
 "service": "jarvis-camera"
}

8. Logging

Each system should write its own logs:

D:\Project AI\jarvis-logs

Example:

core.log
gui.log
voice.log
camera.log
memory.log
local-agent.log

This makes fault-finding much easier.

9. Security recommendation

The local agent must not have unlimited control.

Use permission levels:

Level 1: Read only
Level 2: Create files
Level 3: Modify files
Level 4: Run commands
Level 5: System changes

Anything Level 3 or above should ask for approval.

10. Strong recommendation

Use this build order:

jarvis-core

jarvis-gui

jarvis-memory

jarvis-voice

jarvis-camera

jarvis-local-agent

jarvis-internet

Update and backup system

Do not build camera or local computer control first.

Start with a stable AI core and GUI.

Codex plugin has been installed

Excellent. That will be useful, but it's important to understand what Codex is and isn't.

Codex is a coding assistant, not the AI itself. Think of it as one of the engineers helping to build JARVIS, rather than JARVIS itself.

Project JARVIS AI

I think we should approach this as if we were a professional software company building a commercial product.

Our Objectives

We will build a production-quality AI assistant that is:

Cross-platform (Windows first, then Android, then iPhone)

Modular (every subsystem independent)

Expandable

Fault tolerant

Version controlled

Self-documenting

Secure

Low cost

Easy to update

Future-proof

One additional recommendation

I recommend we create a Project Constitution.

This becomes the "law" that every future piece of code must follow.

For example:

Rule 1

Every subsystem must operate independently.

No subsystem may directly depend on another except through documented APIs.

Rule 2

If any subsystem crashes:

Core continues

GUI continues

Voice continues if possible

Only that subsystem restarts.

Rule 3

Every module must contain

README.md
CHANGELOG.md
requirements.txt
config.json
logs\
tests\
src\

without exception.

Rule 4

Every release receives a version number.

Example

v0.1.0

Major

v1.0.0

Hotfix

v1.0.1

Rule 5

Every code change must include

Git Commit

Changelog entry

Unit test

Health check

Rule 6

No hardcoded settings.

Everything belongs inside

config.json

or

.env

Rule 7

Nothing may be deleted automatically.

Everything first goes to

Recycle
Archive
Backup

Rule 8

The AI never edits production code directly.

Instead:

Read code
Suggest improvement
Generate Patch
You approve
Patch applied
Tests run
Git Commit
Restart service

This gives you complete control while still benefiting from AI-assisted development.

Another recommendation

Every subsystem should expose the same API.

For example:

GET /health
GET /status
GET /version
POST /start
POST /stop
POST /restart

That makes automation much easier.

Logging

I would go beyond normal logging.

Each module should have:

Activity Log
Error Log
Performance Log
AI Decision Log
Security Log

You'll always know:

what happened

why it happened

how long it took

I would also add an Event Bus

Instead of modules talking directly:

Camera
 \
Voice -----> Event Bus -----> Core
 /
Memory

Advantages:

Easier debugging

Less coupling

Faster expansion

Modules can be replaced without affecting others

My biggest recommendation

I think we should avoid building "a chatbot with features."

Instead, build an AI Operating System.

That changes the architecture completely.

Instead of:

ChatGPT

we build:

Project JARVIS AI
 AI Core
 Memory Engine
 Vision Engine
 Voice Engine
 Reasoning Engine
 Automation Engine
 Device Manager
 Internet Manager
 Security Manager
 Plugin Manager
 Learning Engine
 Avatar Engine
 GUI

Notice that chat becomes just one interface to the AI, alongside voice, camera, automation, and other capabilities.

One final recommendation

I recommend we treat this as a long-term engineering project rather than a series of prompts. We should establish:

A complete architecture document.

A development roadmap with milestones.

Coding standards.

Testing standards.

Release procedures.

Security policies.

API specifications.

Deployment documentation.

A project backlog and feature tracker.

This gives us a professional foundation that can scale from a personal assistant into a much more capable AI platform over time.

I think this approach will produce something far closer to the "JARVIS" vision than simply integrating a collection of AI services.

Agreed on all recommendations, please add these to the development documents. As we progress after each phase we should stop and review to make sure best practice has been followed before moving on. Do not hesitate to recommend any improvements to process and system as we move forward.

I completely agree with this approach. In fact, I'd like to make one adjustment to the project methodology.

Rather than treating me as simply a code generator, I will act as Chief AI Architect, Lead Software Engineer, Solution Architect, Security Architect, QA Lead, and Technical Documentation Lead throughout the project. That means I won't just build what you ask-I will actively challenge decisions when there is a better approach, identify risks, recommend improvements, and ensure the project remains maintainable and scalable.

Project JARVIS AI Development Charter

I consider this our "constitution." Every future design and code decision must comply with it unless we formally decide to amend it.

1. Project Vision

Build a modular, production-quality AI operating system inspired by JARVIS that is:

Cross-platform

Voice-first

Vision capable

Extensible

Self-documenting

Secure by design

Highly resilient

Cost efficient

Easy to maintain

Capable of continuous improvement through controlled AI assistance

2. Development Philosophy

Every phase must satisfy:

Works correctly

Secure

Documented

Tested

Version Controlled

Recoverable

Modular

Future Proof

If any of those fail, the phase is not complete.

3. Phase Gate Reviews (New)

I strongly recommend introducing formal review gates.

No phase progresses until it passes.

Each gate will include:

Architecture Review

Does it still fit the long-term architecture?

Has unnecessary complexity been introduced?

Can it be simplified?

Security Review

Any unnecessary permissions?

Any secrets exposed?

Any vulnerabilities?

Principle of Least Privilege followed?

Performance Review

Startup time

Memory usage

CPU usage

API response times

Cost implications

QA Review

Unit Tests

Integration Tests

Manual Tests

Edge Cases

Documentation Review

Everything updated:

README

Architecture

Changelog

API docs

Installation docs

Code Review

Naming standards

Formatting

Error handling

Logging

Comments

Test coverage

Release Review

Only after all previous reviews pass.

4. Living Documentation

Documentation should never fall behind the code.

We'll maintain:

Project Constitution
Architecture Guide
Developer Guide
Deployment Guide
Installation Guide
User Manual
API Documentation
Testing Guide
Security Guide
Backup Guide
Recovery Guide
Roadmap
Release Notes
Changelog
Coding Standards
Plugin Development Guide

Documentation will be treated as part of the deliverable, not an afterthought.

5. Coding Standards

Every subsystem must include:

README.md
CHANGELOG.md
LICENSE
requirements.txt
config.json
.env.example
tests/
docs/
src/
logs/

No exceptions.

6. Git Standards

Every change must:

Create feature branch

Pass tests

Update documentation

Update changelog

Merge into Development

Review

Merge into Main

7. Automatic Documentation

One recommendation I'd like to add:

Every API should automatically generate documentation.

For example:

http://localhost/docs

Always reflects current code.

8. AI Safety Rules

The AI will never:

Delete code automatically

Rewrite production code automatically

Change configuration without approval

Execute high-risk commands without approval

Update itself without approval

Instead it will:

Analyse

Recommend

Generate Patch

Run Tests

Await Approval

Deploy

9. Recovery Standards

Every subsystem must be able to:

Restart independently

Recover automatically

Restore previous version

Report failure

Continue operating if another subsystem fails

10. Monitoring

Everything measurable should be measured.

Examples:

CPU

RAM

GPU

API latency

Voice latency

Vision FPS

Memory database size

Number of remembered objects

Plugin health

Internet latency

LLM response time

Costs

11. Future Plugin System

I recommend planning for plugins from day one.

Future additions should require no modification of the core.

Examples:

Weather Plugin
Email Plugin
Calendar Plugin
Home Assistant Plugin
Spotify Plugin
Teams Plugin
GitHub Plugin
Drone Plugin
Robot Plugin

12. Development Workflow

Every feature will follow this lifecycle:

Requirements

Design

Architecture Review

Build

Unit Testing

Integration Testing

Documentation

Security Review

Performance Review

Phase Gate Approval

Release

Retrospective

13. My Ongoing Responsibilities

As the project evolves, I will proactively:

Recommend architectural improvements.

Suggest technologies that reduce cost or complexity.

Identify security risks before implementation.

Highlight opportunities to improve performance.

Advise when a proposed feature should be deferred to a later phase.

Recommend refactoring when technical debt begins to accumulate.

Ensure documentation remains current.

Keep the project aligned with the long-term vision.

If I believe there is a significantly better solution than the one we're pursuing, I will explain the trade-offs and recommend the alternative rather than simply following the original plan.

One additional proposal

I'd like to introduce one more concept that I believe will add significant value: a Project Management Office (PMO) layer for Project JARVIS AI.

This would include:

Product Backlog with prioritised features.

Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) to document important technical decisions and why they were made.

Risk Register to track technical, security, cost, and delivery risks.

Issue Register for defects and known limitations.

Milestone Plan with defined phase objectives and acceptance criteria.

Technical Debt Register to capture intentional shortcuts that should be addressed later.

Decision Log so we always know why a particular design choice was made.

These practices are commonly used on large engineering programmes and will help keep Project JARVIS AI organised and maintainable as it grows in scope. I believe they'll be especially valuable for a long-term project like this, where the architecture will evolve over time.

Once again i agree with the recommendations. From a perspective of cash flow - where i would like to keep cost to a minimum if there is a paid solution that we offer better results please suggest it. I currently use mainly Windows 10 on most of my devices so please take that into consideration. I also think we need to have a wish list of What we must have and is critical and also a nice to have which is low priority.

Excellent. I think we're setting this project up the right way. One thing I'd like to establish now is a guiding principle for every technology decision.

We will always choose the solution that provides the best balance of functionality, reliability, maintainability, and cost-not simply the cheapest option.

If spending 10/month saves us 100 hours of development time or significantly improves the user experience, I'll recommend it and explain why. Conversely, if an open-source solution provides 95% of the value for free, that will be my default recommendation.

Project JARVIS AI - Cost Strategy

I propose a simple decision framework for all components:

Tier

Cost

When we choose it

Free

0

Default if it meets our quality standards.

Low Cost

5-20/month

Recommended when it delivers a significant improvement in reliability or capability.

Professional

20-100/month

Only when it unlocks important features or saves substantial development effort.

Enterprise

100+/month

Avoid unless there is a compelling future requirement.

Every recommendation I make will include:

Free option

Recommended option

Professional option (if applicable)

Why I recommend it

Estimated ongoing cost

Windows 10 as the Primary Platform

This is an important design decision.

We'll officially support:

Priority

Platform

Status

1

Windows 10

Primary development platform

2

Windows 11

Fully supported

3

Android

Phase 2

4

iPhone/iPad

Phase 3

5

Linux

Optional

6

macOS

Optional

That means:

All scripts will be written and tested on Windows 10 first.

Folder paths, installers, and services will be Windows-first.

Where possible, we'll keep the code cross-platform so future support is straightforward.

Product Backlog

I think this is one of the most important documents we'll maintain.

We'll divide it into four categories rather than two.

Tier 1 - Critical (Must Have)

Without these, it is not JARVIS.

Examples:

AI Core

GUI

Voice

Wake Word

Long-Term Memory

Internet Access

Local Device Control

Plugin System

Version Control

Logging

Security

Health Monitoring

Update System

These are non-negotiable.

Tier 2 - High Priority (Should Have)

These add major value but don't block the core vision.

Examples:

Camera Vision

Face Recognition

Object Recognition

Calendar Integration

Email Integration

Smart Home Integration

Mobile App

Multi-device Synchronisation

Tier 3 - Nice to Have (Could Have)

These enhance the experience.

Examples:

Animated Avatar Expressions

Weather Effects in the GUI

Custom Themes

Music Control

News Summaries

Daily Briefing

Ambient Sounds

Personality Profiles

Tier 4 - Future Vision (Won't Build Initially)

These are aspirational features we'll keep on the roadmap.

Examples:

Robotics Integration

Drone Control

Autonomous Task Planning

AR Glasses

Wearable Integration

Vehicle Integration

Distributed AI Processing

Multi-Agent Collaboration

This keeps us focused while preserving long-term ambition.

Additional Recommendation: Project Board

Instead of just a to-do list, I recommend a proper project board with the following columns:

Ideas
Wish List
Requirements
Architecture Review
Ready for Development
In Development
Testing
Documentation Review
Security Review
Approved
Released

This gives us a clear, disciplined workflow.

Additional Recommendation: Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)

Every significant technical decision should be recorded.

For example:

Why we chose a particular AI model.

Why we selected a specific database.

Why we built a custom memory system.

Why we adopted a particular GUI framework.

This prevents us from revisiting the same decisions months later without context.

My Recommendation for the AI "Brain"

One architectural suggestion I'd like to make now:

Don't tie Project JARVIS AI to a single AI model or provider.

Instead, build an abstraction layer so the AI engine can switch between providers.

For example:

AI Core
 AI Provider Manager
 OpenAI Local LLM Future Provider

Benefits:

Avoid vendor lock-in.

Switch providers if pricing changes.

Run local models for privacy-sensitive tasks.

Use cloud models only when they add value.

Future-proof the system as AI evolves.

This flexibility will be invaluable over the lifetime of the project.

One More Recommendation: Development Principles

I'd like to adopt these as guiding principles:

Modular First - Every subsystem should be independently deployable.

API First - All communication through documented APIs.

Security by Design - Security considered from the beginning, not added later.

Offline First - Core features should continue to work without an internet connection where practical.

Cloud Optional - Cloud services enhance the experience but should not be mandatory for the assistant to function.

Human in Control - High-impact actions always require your approval.

Observability by Default - Logging, metrics, and health monitoring built into every subsystem.

Document as We Build - Documentation is updated alongside the code.

Continuous Improvement - Each phase ends with a retrospective and lessons learned.

Keep It Simple - Prefer simple, maintainable solutions over unnecessary complexity.

A Final Strategic Suggestion

Rather than thinking of this as "building JARVIS," I suggest we define a Minimum Lovable Product (MLP) rather than just a Minimum Viable Product (MVP).

The MLP should be something you genuinely enjoy using every day. That means from the very first usable version it should feel responsive, visually appealing, and genuinely helpful-even if it only has a handful of capabilities. Building that positive daily experience early will give us a stronger foundation than trying to implement every possible feature before it's enjoyable to use.

Again excellent ideas, i would also like to add for it to be family friendly as well - one of the things i would like to use it for is from my wife and daughter to be able to ask for assistance for example my daughters exam work and course work, while my wife can ask for IT help or help in designing posters or advertisement for her work. For me it will be able assist in IT related work, cybersecurity and writing best practice and policy guides.

I actually think what you've just described changes the project in a very positive way.

We're no longer building a personal AI assistant.

We're building a Family AI Operating System with personalised assistants for each family member.

That is a much better long-term architecture because it allows the AI to adapt to each person while still sharing a common core.

New Vision Statement

Project JARVIS AI is a secure, modular, family-friendly AI operating system that provides personalised assistance for each authorised user while maintaining a shared knowledge base and respecting privacy.

This has several important implications for the design.

User Profiles

Instead of one "Jarvis", we'll have one AI core that serves multiple users.

For example:

JARVIS Core
 Robert Wife Daughter

Each user has:

Their own memory.

Their own preferences.

Their own conversations.

Their own permissions.

Their own skills.

Their own dashboard.

Their own learning history.

The AI core is shared, but the experience is personalised.

Shared Knowledge vs Private Knowledge

This is an area where we need to be very deliberate. I recommend we introduce knowledge scopes.

Personal Knowledge

Visible only to the individual user.

Examples:

Personal reminders.

Private notes.

Draft documents.

Exam preparation.

Work-related conversations.

Family Knowledge

Shared with all family members.

Examples:

Shopping lists.

Family calendar.

Household reminders.

Recipes.

Holiday planning.

Shared documents.

System Knowledge

Used internally by JARVIS.

Examples:

Device information.

Connected services.

Installed plugins.

System configuration.

Health status.

This separation gives us flexibility while protecting privacy.

Family Roles

I recommend defining roles from the beginning.

Role

Permissions

Administrator

Full system control.

Adult

Access to personal and family features.

Child

Age-appropriate features with restrictions.

Guest

Limited temporary access.

As the administrator, you'll control who can do what.

Age-Appropriate Assistance

For your daughter, JARVIS should act as a tutor, not a shortcut.

For example, instead of simply giving answers, it should:

Explain concepts.

Ask guiding questions.

Provide practice exercises.

Encourage critical thinking.

Help with revision plans.

This aligns with educational best practices and helps develop understanding rather than dependency.

Specialised AI "Modes"

Rather than trying to make one personality do everything, I suggest we create specialist modes that share the same underlying AI.

For example:

Robert's Workspace

Optimised for:

IT strategy.

Cybersecurity.

Microsoft 365.

Azure.

Policy writing.

ITIL.

Service management.

Software development.

AI architecture.

Wife's Workspace

Optimised for:

Graphic design.

Posters.

Social media.

Marketing.

Office productivity.

IT troubleshooting.

Document creation.

Daughter's Workspace

Optimised for:

Homework support.

Exam preparation.

Research.

Study planning.

Time management.

Revision quizzes.

The AI behaves differently depending on who is using it, but it remains one unified system.

Family Dashboard

The GUI could include widgets tailored to each user.

For example:

Family Dashboard

Calendar.

Weather.

Shopping list.

Recent announcements.

Device health.

AI status.

Personal Dashboard

Current tasks.

Notes.

Recent conversations.

Favourite tools.

Frequently used plugins.

Plugin Categories

This reinforces the idea of modularity.

Productivity

Calendar.

Email.

Microsoft 365.

Notes.

Education

Flashcards.

Revision planner.

Quiz generator.

Research assistant.

Creative

Poster design.

Image generation.

Social media.

Document layouts.

IT

PowerShell.

Microsoft 365 administration.

Active Directory.

Networking.

Cybersecurity tools.

Home

Shopping lists.

Smart home.

Recipes.

Family calendar.

Family Safety

Because children will be using the system, I recommend we adopt Safety by Design.

Key principles:

Age-appropriate responses.

Content filtering where appropriate.

No sharing of one user's private information with another.

Clear separation of personal and shared memories.

Parental oversight for child accounts.

One Recommendation I'd Like to Add

I think we should introduce a Skills Framework.

Rather than hard-coding capabilities into the AI, we'll define them as skills.

Examples:

Conversation.

Vision.

Voice.

Coding.

Cybersecurity.

Teaching.

Creative writing.

Graphic design.

Planning.

Automation.

Each skill can evolve independently and be enabled or disabled for different users.

This makes the system more flexible and easier to extend over time.

Project Governance Update

I'd like to add one final guiding principle:

Every feature must answer one question before it is added: "Does this make JARVIS genuinely more useful for the family?"

If the answer is no, it doesn't go into the current release. It stays on the backlog until it delivers clear value.

My Final Recommendation

I believe we're now beyond a simple software project. We should treat this as a multi-year engineering programme with a long-term vision. That doesn't mean it will take years to become useful-we'll aim to have a genuinely helpful Version 1 much sooner-but every architectural decision should support where we want the platform to be in three to five years.

With that mindset, we'll focus on building a robust foundation first, then layer capabilities on top without needing to rewrite major parts of the system. That approach gives us the best chance of creating something that remains useful, maintainable, and enjoyable for your whole family over the long term.

Excellent - i think what would be helpful is when we are able to get to a certain phrase we should front load the system with personal information on each family member ahead of time so when they interact initially there is some knowledge of the person - in regards to accessing the system i would like the AI to use visual recognition where possible, with two other fall backs 1. Phone authentication fall back and then password.

I'll add this as a new identity, onboarding, and family profile requirement. This is important enough to become part of the core security architecture, not just a later feature.

Agreed. I recommend we add this as a formal requirement:

Family Profile & Identity System

1. Pre-loaded family profiles

Before each family member uses JARVIS, we create a profile containing safe, useful information.

Example:

Name
Preferred name
Role: Admin / Adult / Child
Interests
Work/school needs
Learning style
Allowed features
Restricted features
Voice preference
Privacy level

This means JARVIS can give personalised help from the first conversation.

2. Authentication order

Recommended login flow:

1. Visual recognition
 if unavailable or uncertain
2. Phone authentication
 if unavailable
3. Password / PIN

3. Visual recognition rules

Face recognition should be used for convenience, not as the only security method.

Rules:

Face recognised with high confidence allow access
Face uncertain ask for phone approval
Face not recognised ask for password/PIN

4. Phone fallback

Best options:

Windows Hello / Microsoft Authenticator
Passkey
Push approval to phone
QR code login

5. Password fallback

Password should be last resort.

Minimum recommendation:

Strong password or PIN
Account lockout after failed attempts
Admin reset option
Audit log of failed logins

6. Child account safety

For your daughter's profile:

No unrestricted internet mode
No private adult data access
Educational help, not answer-only cheating
Parent/admin visibility controls
Age-appropriate content filtering

7. Important security rule

JARVIS should never say:

"You are Robert"

based only on face recognition.

Instead:

"I think you are Robert. Please confirm."

For sensitive actions, it should always require stronger proof.

New development document to add

D:\Project AI\jarvis-docs\IDENTITY_AND_FAMILY_PROFILES.md

This document will cover:

Family profiles
Authentication flow
Face recognition
Phone fallback
Password fallback
Child safety
Privacy rules
Permission levels
Audit logging
Profile onboarding

I also recommend adding this to the Critical/Must Have list:

User Identity & Permission System
Family Profile Management
Authentication Fallback System
Child Safety Controls

While we are talking about security - i would in the future like to be able have the AI connect to additional cameras placed around the house (not in bedrooms or private area's) and outside and be able to monitor them in real time. If we can have it monitoring the network and local devices for cyber threats and vulnerabilities that would be a nice to have.

Agreed. I would add this as a future security and safety layer, not part of Version 1.

New Future Module: Home Security & Cyber Monitoring

1. Camera monitoring

This should become a separate subsystem:

D:\Project AI\jarvis-home-security

Purpose:

Connect to approved home cameras
View live feeds
Detect motion
Detect people
Detect unknown faces
Detect unusual activity
Send alerts
Keep audit logs

Important privacy rule:

No cameras in bedrooms, bathrooms, or private areas.

I would also add:

Visible consent rules for family members
Clear camera locations list
Admin-only camera setup
Recording retention limits

2. Recommended camera approach

Best future setup:

IP cameras
RTSP video streams
Local processing where possible
No cloud recording unless you approve it

Why:

Lower monthly cost
Better privacy
Works locally
Less dependent on vendor subscriptions

3. Camera security rules

The AI should not have unlimited access.

Permission levels:

View live feed Adult/Admin
Review recordings Admin only
Add camera Admin only
Delete recordings Admin approval required
Export footage Admin approval required

4. Cyber monitoring module

This should also be separate:

D:\Project AI\jarvis-cyber-monitor

Purpose:

Monitor local devices
Check network health
Identify unknown devices
Flag weak security settings
Check open ports
Watch for suspicious behaviour
Recommend fixes

5. Safe cyber monitoring scope

Allowed:

Scan your own home network
Check your own devices
Look for missing updates
Detect unknown devices
Check firewall status
Review antivirus status
Recommend security improvements

Not allowed:

Attacking devices
Bypassing passwords
Intercepting private messages
Scanning networks you do not own
Automated exploitation

6. Priority level

I would classify these like this:

Feature

Priority

Security architecture

Critical

Permission system

Critical

Audit logging

Critical

Camera integration

High / Future

Real-time camera monitoring

Nice to Have / Future

Network monitoring

Nice to Have

Vulnerability recommendations

Nice to Have

Automated remediation

Future / Admin approval only

7. Recommended document update

Add:

D:\Project AI\jarvis-docs\HOME_SECURITY_AND_CYBER_MONITORING.md

This should cover:

Camera placement policy
Privacy rules
Approved camera locations
Camera permissions
Recording policy
Alert rules
Cyber monitoring scope
Home network scanning rules
Device inventory
Vulnerability checks
Admin approval requirements

8. Strong recommendation

For the cyber side, JARVIS should be an advisor first, not an automatic fixer.

Example:

JARVIS detects:
"Unknown device joined the network."
JARVIS recommends:
"Block this device on the router?"
You approve:
"Yes, block it."

This keeps the system useful without becoming dangerous or disruptive.

Agreed JARVIS should only recommend and suggest changes and fixes - but in a situation when there is danger and an immediate decision is need we need to allow some flexibility.

I completely agree, and I think this is one of the most important architectural decisions we'll make.

However, I would refine it slightly.

The guiding principle should not be:

"JARVIS can make decisions in dangerous situations."

Instead, it should be:

"JARVIS may execute only pre-authorised emergency actions that have been explicitly approved in advance by the Administrator."

That small change gives us the flexibility you want without creating an AI that has unrestricted authority.

Project JARVIS AI - Emergency Action Framework (EAF)

I recommend introducing an Emergency Action Framework that governs what the AI is permitted to do automatically.

Principle

JARVIS never gains new authority during an emergency.

Instead, it follows a set of emergency actions that you have previously approved.

Emergency Levels

I suggest a five-level system.

Level

Description

AI Action

Level 0

Information

Notify only.

Level 1

Low Risk

Recommend action.

Level 2

Medium Risk

Recommend and prepare the action.

Level 3

High Risk

Execute only if pre-authorised.

Level 4

Critical

Execute approved emergency actions immediately and notify the Administrator.

Example: Cyber Attack

Imagine ransomware is detected.

Instead of asking:

"Should I disconnect the computer?"

(which could take too long)

The policy could already state:

If ransomware confidence > 95%
Disconnect affected PC from the network
Stop file sharing
Prevent lateral movement
Notify Administrator
Create incident log
Begin evidence collection

Everything after that waits for your approval.

Example: Unknown Person on Camera

Suppose someone enters your garden at 3:00 AM.

The policy might allow JARVIS to:

Record video

Turn on external lights

Notify your phone

Save camera footage

Continue tracking movement

But not:

Call the police automatically

Unlock doors

Speak to the individual

Those would remain manual decisions unless you explicitly create a policy allowing them.

Example: House Fire

If future integrations include smoke or heat sensors:

JARVIS could automatically:

Notify every family member.

Turn on all house lights.

Unlock smart locks (if you choose).

Shut down non-essential electrical devices (where supported).

Display evacuation guidance.

Call pre-approved emergency contacts if you've configured that behaviour.

Again, only because you've approved those actions beforehand.

Emergency Policies

I recommend we introduce an Emergency Policy Engine.

Each policy would define:

Trigger conditions.

Confidence threshold.

Approved actions.

Notification recipients.

Audit requirements.

This makes the system predictable and auditable.

Administrator Control

Only the Administrator should be able to:

Create emergency policies.

Modify them.

Enable or disable them.

Review their history.

No other account can change these rules.

Full Audit Trail

Every automatic emergency action should generate a permanent record.

For example:

Date/Time
Emergency Type
Confidence Level
Trigger Source
Actions Performed
Reason
Policy Used
Outcome
Administrator Notified

Nothing is hidden or discarded.

A "Guardian Mode"

I'd also propose a named operating mode called Guardian Mode.

Guardian Mode is not the AI "taking over." It's a controlled operating mode in which JARVIS is permitted to execute only the emergency actions defined in your approved policies.

Examples include:

Home security monitoring.

Cybersecurity monitoring.

Health of connected devices.

Environmental monitoring (smoke, water leaks, temperature).

Family safety alerts.

This keeps emergency behaviour separate from normal day-to-day assistance.

One Area Where I Would Push Back

There is one capability I would deliberately avoid:

JARVIS deciding that "something feels dangerous" and inventing a response.

Instead, every automated action should be based on:

Defined triggers.

Known confidence thresholds.

Pre-approved policies.

Logged decisions.

That keeps the system explainable, predictable, and trustworthy.

A Long-Term Vision

I think we should eventually divide JARVIS into operational modes, each with clearly defined responsibilities:

Mode

Purpose

Assistant Mode

Conversation, research, productivity, family support.

Guardian Mode

Security, safety, emergency monitoring, approved automatic actions.

Engineer Mode

IT administration, coding assistance, infrastructure, cybersecurity recommendations.

Tutor Mode

Education, revision, coursework support, quizzes, learning plans.

Creative Mode

Posters, advertisements, presentations, image generation, writing assistance.

All of these modes would use the same underlying AI Core, Memory Engine, Identity System, and Security Framework. They would differ only in the tools, permissions, and policies available.

An Additional Recommendation

I believe we should make Guardian Mode an optional module that can be enabled or disabled independently of the rest of the system. This aligns with one of our core architectural principles: every subsystem should have as few dependencies as possible. If Guardian Mode is offline for maintenance or removed entirely, the Assistant, Tutor, Creative, and Engineer modes should continue to operate normally. This modularity will make the platform easier to maintain, test, and extend over time.

I would like to add a feature that if a family members requires assistance that Emergency mode can be activated via voice command or other manner.?

Yes - this should be added.

I recommend calling it:

Emergency Help Activation

This would sit inside Guardian Mode.

Purpose

Allow an authorised family member to trigger help quickly using:

Voice command
Phone button
GUI emergency button
Wearable device
Keyboard shortcut
Smart speaker command

Example voice commands

"JARVIS, I need help"
"JARVIS, emergency"
"JARVIS, call for help"
"JARVIS, I feel unsafe"
"JARVIS, alert Robert"

Emergency flow

Family member activates emergency
JARVIS confirms identity if possible
JARVIS asks what type of help is needed
If no response, escalate automatically
Notify approved emergency contacts
Start audio/video logging if approved
Display emergency guidance
Create incident log

Emergency types

Medical help
Personal safety
Home security
Fire / smoke
Cybersecurity incident
Child needs parent
General urgent help

Important rule

JARVIS should not rely on perfect voice recognition in an emergency.

So we should support fallback triggers:

Emergency button in app
PIN phrase
Repeated wake phrase
Panic shortcut on PC
Phone lock-screen shortcut
Smart home button

Pre-approved actions

You would decide in advance what JARVIS is allowed to do.

Example:

If daughter says: "JARVIS, I need help"
Allowed actions:
- Notify Robert
- Notify mother
- Show calm instructions
- Start incident timer
- Keep voice channel open
- Log event

For more serious emergencies:

If anyone says: "JARVIS, emergency"
Allowed actions:
- Notify all adults
- Turn on house lights
- Record from approved cameras
- Send location if on phone
- Escalate if no adult responds

Safety controls

I recommend these controls:

Cancel phrase:
"JARVIS, cancel emergency"
Duress phrase:
"JARVIS, everything is fine"

The duress phrase sounds normal but secretly keeps the alert active.

Priority level

Add to backlog as:

Critical:
Emergency Help Activation Framework
High Priority:
Voice emergency trigger
Phone emergency trigger
GUI panic button
Future:
Wearable trigger
Smart speaker trigger
Automated escalation

D:\Project AI\jarvis-docs\GUARDIAN_MODE.md

ok have a quick review of what we have covered and make sure that we have captured as much information to make a start on the framework and documentation. If you think additional plugins or software is required to enable you work more streamlined please inform me.

After reviewing everything we've discussed, I think we're in a very strong position. In fact, I think we've done something that many software projects fail to do-we've spent time defining the vision and architecture before writing code. That investment will save us a great deal of time later.

That said, after reviewing the entire conversation as an architect rather than a developer, I think we should expand the project slightly before writing a single line of code.

My Assessment

I would currently score the project as follows:

Area

Completion

Vision

100%

Architecture Principles

95%

Security Principles

95%

Development Process

95%

Family Requirements

100%

Emergency Framework

90%

Documentation Strategy

90%

AI Architecture

80%

Technical Architecture

75%

Deployment Architecture

60%

Data Architecture

40%

Overall readiness to begin development: approximately 85%.

I would like to raise that to 95% before we start building.

What I Think Is Still Missing

1. System Architecture (Highest Priority)

We need a complete architectural blueprint.

Not just boxes, but a document defining:

GUI
AI Core
Memory
Voice
Vision
Guardian
Local Agent
Internet
Plugin Manager
Database
Logging
Monitoring

This becomes the "master blueprint."

2. Data Architecture

One of the biggest projects is actually the data.

We need to define what JARVIS remembers.

For example:

Family
Devices
Rooms
Objects
Contacts
Passwords (never stored in plain text)
Preferences
Calendar
Emails
Projects
Policies
Documents
Photos
Conversations
Learning History
Skills
Plugins

I recommend designing the data model before implementation.

3. AI Memory Architecture

This is where most AI assistants fall short.

I propose multiple memory layers.

Working Memory
(Seconds)
Conversation Memory
(Hours)
Personal Memory
(Months)
Family Memory
Project Memory
System Memory
Knowledge Base
Archive

This layered approach will make JARVIS feel much more coherent over time.

4. Plugin Development Kit (PDK)

I think we should design a Plugin SDK before writing plugins.

Every future plugin should follow the same template.

That way, adding new capabilities becomes straightforward.

5. GUI Design System

The GUI deserves its own design specification.

It should define:

Layout

Colours

Animations

Typography

Widget behaviour

Accessibility

Dark/Light themes

3D avatar interactions

This ensures consistency as the system grows.

6. AI Personality Specification

This is an area that often gets overlooked.

We should define how JARVIS behaves.

Examples:

Calm

Professional

Family friendly

Humorous (optional)

Formal when writing policies

Supportive when tutoring

Direct during emergencies

This prevents inconsistent behaviour.

7. Testing Framework

I'd like automated testing from day one.

Every subsystem should have:

Unit tests

Integration tests

API tests

Security tests

Performance tests

Recovery tests

8. Deployment Pipeline

Eventually, updates should be almost effortless.

Git
Automatic Build
Tests
Documentation Check
Version Number
Package
Backup
Deploy
Restart
Health Check

My Biggest Recommendation

I think we should add one final foundational document before coding begins:

This becomes the authoritative source for the entire project.

Scope

Architecture

Every other document would reference this specification.

My Proposal for Phase 0

Rather than jumping straight into development, I propose a dedicated Phase 0 - Foundation & Governance.

The objective is to produce a complete, professional project foundation. Deliverables would include:

The Master Specification.

Complete folder structure under D:\Project AI.

Git repository strategy.

Coding standards.

Documentation templates.

Architecture diagrams.

Initial backlog.

Risk register.

Security policies.

Update and rollback strategy.

Development environment configuration.

Only when Phase 0 passes its review gate would we begin Phase 1 (Core Architecture).

One Final Suggestion

Given the ambition of this project, I'd also like to maintain a Decision Journal. Every significant recommendation I make-whether it's choosing a technology, changing an architecture, or deferring a feature-will be recorded along with the rationale and trade-offs. Six months or two years from now, we'll still know why a decision was made, which is invaluable for a long-lived engineering programme. I believe this discipline will make Project JARVIS AI much easier to evolve and maintain.

Agreed, we also must not be afraid to say no to a request of add-on - we need to pragmatic and look at the bigger picture as well as the smaller picture - if its to ambitious we need to acknowledge that and move to the bottom of the list.

I couldn't agree more. In fact, I think you've just identified one of the biggest reasons ambitious software projects fail: they never say "no."

I'd like to make this one of the core principles of Project JARVIS AI.

New Project Principle

Pragmatism Over Ambition

Every proposed feature must earn its place in the project.

Just because something is technically possible doesn't mean it belongs in the current phase.

For every new idea, we'll ask:

Does this align with the long-term vision?

Does it improve the user experience?

Does it introduce unnecessary complexity?

Can it be delivered reliably with our current architecture?

Is this the right time to build it?

If the answer to any of these is "no," it goes into the backlog rather than the current release.

The "JARVIS Challenge"

I'd like to introduce a practice I call the JARVIS Challenge.

Whenever either of us suggests a feature, we deliberately challenge it before accepting it.

We'll ask:

What problem does this solve?

Is there a simpler solution?

Does it fit our architecture?

What are the security implications?

What are the maintenance implications?

What is the estimated cost (time and money)?

Can it be implemented as a plugin instead of modifying the core?

Can it wait until a later phase?

If it survives those questions, it moves forward.

Project Governance Rule

I'd like to formalise another rule:

The project roadmap is driven by value, not enthusiasm.

Many exciting ideas will emerge-and that's a good thing-but we'll prioritise based on the value they deliver, not how interesting they are.

Feature Classification

Every proposed feature will be assigned one of these statuses:

Status

Meaning

Approved

Fits the current phase and roadmap.

Deferred

Valuable, but better suited to a later phase.

Experimental

Worth researching before committing.

Rejected

Doesn't align with the vision or introduces excessive complexity.

Superseded

Replaced by a better solution.

This gives us a disciplined way to manage ideas without losing them.

Complexity Budget

This is a concept used in large software programmes.

Every release has a limited "complexity budget."

For example:

One major architectural feature.

Two medium features.

Several small improvements.

Once the budget is spent, everything else waits for the next release.

This helps keep each release stable and manageable.

My Responsibility

I want to make a commitment to you about how I'll support this project.

I won't simply agree with every suggestion because it's technically feasible.

If I believe an idea:

adds unnecessary complexity,

creates security risks,

increases technical debt,

makes the system harder to maintain,

or doesn't fit the current phase,

I'll explain why, present the trade-offs, and recommend whether it should be approved, deferred, or rejected.

That means I'll sometimes say:

"This is a good idea, but not for Version 1."

or

"This is architecturally unsound."

or

"There's a simpler solution that achieves 90% of the value."

I believe that's the role of a technical architect, and it's how we'll protect the long-term quality of the project.

The 90% Rule

I'd also like to adopt a principle I've found very effective:

If a solution delivers 90% of the value for 20% of the effort, it should usually be our first implementation.

We'll always build with future expansion in mind, but we won't over-engineer early versions.

Definition of Done (DoD)

Every feature must meet the following before it's considered complete:

Functional requirements met.

Code reviewed.

Unit and integration tests pass.

Security review completed.

Performance reviewed.

Documentation updated.

Changelog updated.

Version number assigned.

Recovery tested.

Health checks implemented.

Phase review approved.

If any of these are incomplete, the feature remains "In Development."

Success Metrics

To keep us objective, I'd like to define what success looks like for each phase.

Every phase should have measurable acceptance criteria.

For example, for Phase 1:

AI Core starts successfully in under 10 seconds.

GUI connects to the AI Core.

Health checks pass.

Logs are written correctly.

Version information is displayed.

Services can be stopped and restarted independently.

No Critical or High severity defects remain open.

Only then do we move on.

One Final Addition: The "Architect's Veto"

I'd like to propose one governance mechanism that applies equally to both of us.

Any feature can receive an Architect's Veto if it:

Compromises security.

Breaks our modular architecture.

Creates unacceptable technical debt.

Significantly increases maintenance complexity.

Conflicts with our Project Constitution.

A veto doesn't mean the idea is bad-it simply means it's not appropriate in its current form or at the current stage. It must either be redesigned or deferred.

This gives us a structured way to protect the integrity of the project without losing good ideas.

My Commitment

From this point forward, I'll approach every recommendation with three perspectives simultaneously:

Today's Engineer - Can we build it?

Tomorrow's Architect - Will it still be the right decision in five years?

The Family User - Does it genuinely make JARVIS more useful, safer, and easier to use?

If a proposal satisfies all three, it belongs in Project JARVIS AI. If not, I'll tell you why and recommend the most sensible path forward. Based on everything we've discussed, I believe we now have the right governance, technical discipline, and long-term vision to build something that is ambitious without becoming unmanageable.

Another thing to be aware of is that i develop on a laptop here at work and also a PC at home, any code we write must be able to be shared or implemented on both devices.

Agreed. Add this as a core requirement:

Multi-Device Development Requirement

Work laptop
Home PC

D:\Project AI = standard local folder
.env files = device-specific settings

Rules

D:\Project AI

LOCAL_AGENT_PORT=8010

D:\Project AI\jarvis-config\devices\home-pc.json

Use setup scripts

D:\Project AI\setup-windows.bat
D:\Project AI\update-all.bat
D:\Project AI\health-check.bat

So setup and updates are simple on both machines.

make changes
test

run update-all.bat
run health-check.bat

Project JARVIS AI
A modular, family-friendly AI operating system for Windows-first use, with future phone/device support, voice, GUI, memory, identity, plugins, security, and controlled automation.

The key foundations captured are:

Vision

Family AI assistant inspired by JARVIS.

Useful for you, your wife, and your daughter.

Personalised profiles and permissions.

Platform

Windows 10 primary.

Windows 11 supported.

Development across work laptop and home PC.

D:\Project AI

Architecture

Modular subsystems.

Minimal dependencies between modules.

Plugin-ready design.

GUI

We're not just writing code.

We're designing something that should still be understandable in five years.

Keeping tools separate from the project makes:

More importantly, I want to explain why we stopped here.

A common mistake is to start writing code immediately. Instead, we've created a clean, organised foundation. From this point forward, every file we create will have a logical home.

Architecture Review

I'd like to make our first official Architecture Decision Record (ADR).

ADR-0001 - Folder Structure

Decision:
The D:\Project AI folder will contain project artefacts only. Development tools (VS Code, Git, Python, Node.js, etc.) remain installed in their normal locations and are not copied into the project directory.

Status: Approved [verified]

One Improvement

Looking at our current folders, I think we should make one small enhancement before we go any further.

I'd like to add four new folders:

jarvis-api
jarvis-database
jarvis-monitoring
jarvis-assets

Why?

jarvis-api

This will eventually hold the shared API definitions that every subsystem uses.

Example:

AI Core API

Memory API

Voice API

Camera API

Keeping these separate prevents each module from inventing its own interface.

jarvis-database

At the moment we don't have anywhere for the databases.

Eventually we'll have:

Memory database

Family database

Plugin database

Audit database

Keeping databases separate from the code makes backup and migration much easier.

jarvis-monitoring

This will contain:

Health checks

Performance monitoring

Metrics

Dashboards

Eventually we'll be able to see:

AI Core Healthy
Voice Healthy
Camera Offline
Memory Healthy
Internet Healthy

all in one place.

jarvis-assets

This is where we'll store:

Images

Icons

Sounds

Fonts

3D models

Animations

GUI themes

This keeps media separate from application logic.

Another Recommendation

I also think we should rename one folder.

Currently we have:

jarvis-experiments

I'd like to rename it to:

jarvis-labs

Reason:

"Experiments" sounds temporary.

"Labs" suggests:

Research

Prototypes

AI testing

New ideas

It also fits the overall branding better.

I Have Another Architectural Recommendation

As I've been thinking about everything we've discussed, I believe we should stop referring to modules as "systems."

Instead, let's use the term Services.

For example:

Jarvis Core Service
Jarvis Memory Service
Jarvis Voice Service
Jarvis Vision Service
Jarvis Guardian Service

Why?

Because that's exactly what they are.

Each one:

Starts independently.

Stops independently.

Has its own API.

Has its own logs.

Can be updated independently.

Can be restarted independently.

This terminology also aligns with professional software architecture.

One Final Recommendation (and I think it's the most important one so far)

I want to introduce something called the Project North Star.

Whenever we are unsure whether to add a feature, refactor code, or change the architecture, we ask one question:

"Does this make JARVIS a better family AI operating system?"

If the answer is yes, we consider it.

If the answer is no, or not yet, we defer or reject it.

This one question will help us avoid feature creep and keep the project focused on its true purpose.

ren jarvis-experiments jarvis-labs

I believe that foundation will be one of the biggest contributors to the long-term success of Project JARVIS AI.

Phase 0.1 - Development Environment & Project Structure

Architecture Review

After reviewing everything we've discussed over the last few hours, I have a few observations.

1. We have a vision...

...but we don't yet have a single source of truth.

At the moment, the project knowledge exists across our conversation.

That is not sustainable.

I think our next deliverable should not be code.

It should be the Project JARVIS AI Master Specification.

This becomes the equivalent of a commercial Software Requirements Specification (SRS), Architecture Guide, Governance Manual and Project Constitution combined.

Everything we build from this point forward should reference that document.

2. I think we need one more folder

I'd like to add:

jarvis-tools

Purpose:

This is not for software like VS Code.

It is for Project JARVIS AI's own tools.

For example:

Update Manager

Backup Manager

Installer

Health Checker

Diagnostics

Environment Validator

Configuration Wizard

These are tools we build to manage JARVIS itself.

I think that will become invaluable over time.

3. I would also like one more folder

jarvis-tests

One of my core principles is:

Testing is a first-class citizen.

Tests should never be scattered throughout the project.

This folder will eventually contain:

Unit Tests

Integration Tests

Performance Tests

Security Tests

Recovery Tests

That will make it very easy to automate quality checks later.

4. Repository Strategy

I'd like to make a slight adjustment to my earlier recommendation.

Originally, I suggested three repositories.

I now think we should begin with one repository.

Project-JARVIS-AI

Inside it:

Code

Documentation

Architecture

Tests

Scripts

Why?

You're a solo developer (with me acting as architect and engineering partner).

Multiple repositories add unnecessary complexity at this stage.

If the project grows into a team effort, we can split repositories later.

This is exactly the kind of pragmatic decision we agreed to make.

5. GitHub

I recommend we use GitHub as our remote repository because it gives us:

Automatic backup.

Easy synchronisation between your work laptop and home PC.

Issue tracking.

Project boards.

Release management.

GitHub Actions (later).

Private repositories (free).

This aligns perfectly with your requirement to develop on two devices.

6. Coding Philosophy

I'd like to formally adopt:

"Build once. Reuse everywhere."

If we write a function once, we don't rewrite it elsewhere.

Instead:

GUI
Shared API
Core Service

This will reduce bugs and make maintenance much easier.

7. I think we've reached an important milestone

Up until now we've been:

Planning the project.

From the next phase onwards we'll be:

Building the project.

That transition is significant.

My Proposal for Phase 0.2

Rather than jumping into code, I'd like us to produce a professional project pack.

The deliverables would be:

PJAI-0001 - Master Specification

The governing document for the entire project.

PJAI-0002 - Project Constitution

The rules we've been defining together.

PJAI-0003 - System Architecture

High-level architecture, services, communication, and deployment.

PJAI-0004 - Development Standards

Coding standards, Git workflow, testing, reviews, and release process.

PJAI-0005 - Security Standards

Identity, authentication, permissions, Guardian Mode, emergency framework, audit logging.

PJAI-0006 - Product Backlog

Our prioritised roadmap.

PJAI-0007 - Risk Register

Technical, security, delivery, cost, and operational risks.

PJAI-0008 - Architecture Decision Register (ADR)

Starting with ADR-0001 that we've already created.

One Final Architectural Suggestion

I'd like to add one more principle to our Constitution:

"Every service should be replaceable."

For example, if in three years a better AI provider becomes available, we should be able to replace the AI provider without rewriting the GUI, Memory Service, Voice Service, or Guardian Service.

The same should apply to databases, voice engines, vision models, and even the 3D avatar.

This principle will protect the project from technology changes over its lifetime.

My Recommendation for the Next Session

I think we're now ready to begin what I would consider the official birth of Project JARVIS AI.

The next milestone will not be writing Python code-it will be creating the complete project documentation set. Once that is approved, we will have a governed engineering programme with a clear roadmap, standards, and architecture.

Only then will we start implementing the AI Core Service.

I genuinely believe this is the right order. By investing a little more time in documentation now, we'll dramatically reduce rework, confusion, and architectural drift as the project grows over the coming months and years.

i agree with the above - lets begin

Excellent. I genuinely believe this is the point where Project JARVIS AI officially begins.

I also want to make one commitment before we continue.

My Commitment to This Project

From this point forward I am going to treat this exactly as I would if I had been hired as the Chief Architect on a commercial AI programme.

That means I will be responsible for:

Long-term architecture

Software engineering best practice

Security

AI architecture

Documentation

Project governance

Quality assurance

Technical debt management

Risk management

Challenging decisions when necessary

If I think we're heading in the wrong direction, I will say so and explain why. Likewise, if a better technology becomes available that offers clear benefits, I'll recommend it with a cost/benefit analysis.

instead of:

It's clearer and scales well.

One Change I Want to Make

This is the first time I'm going to use what we agreed to call the Architect's Veto.

I do not think we should write all 20 documents before we start coding.

Earlier, I suggested that because I was thinking like a traditional enterprise architect. After reviewing our situation, I think it introduces unnecessary delay.

Instead, I recommend we create documentation in three waves.

Wave 1 - Foundation (Must Have)
