I'm interested to hear your suggestion. If it helps us reduce the time spent producing controlled documents without compromising AIEMS quality or governance, it's exactly the kind of improvement we should evaluate before continuing.

So the process is currently you produce , i review the we discuss the we format for codex, codex implements, we commit, push then check. Going forward i want to produce all documents in the format for codex to review - not in text boxes to go through individual - one document ready to go into codex - we review the whole document, make changes the copy into codex.

I think this is one of the best improvements you've suggested, and I think it changes my role in a positive way.

At the moment our workflow is:

Me
Produce EWP
You
Review
Discussion
Revise
Codex
Implementation

What you're proposing is:

Me
Produce complete Engineering Work Package (ready for Codex)
You
Engineering review
Approve
Codex
Implementation

That's much closer to how an engineering team works.

I Think My Deliverables Should Change

Instead of producing snippets of text throughout the conversation, I should produce one complete Engineering Work Package.

That work package should contain everything Codex needs:

Engineering objective.

Engineering context.

Repository scope.

Constraints.

Implementation instructions.

Validation requirements.

Completion report.

Success criteria.

Nothing else.

Then we review that single document together.

If we change one sentence, we change it in the work package-not scattered across multiple chat messages.

Even Better...

I think we should go one step further.

Every engineering activity should have exactly one authoritative work package.

For example:

P2-002_ENGINEERING_WORK_PACKAGE.md

That becomes the engineering specification.

We review it.

Approve it.

Then give the entire document to Codex.

There is no translation step.

There is no rewriting.

There is no "copy these three messages".

Just one approved document.

This Changes My Role

I become less of a conversational assistant and more of an engineering author.

My job becomes producing high-quality engineering artefacts that are ready for implementation.

That's actually much closer to the role of a Chief Engineering Advisor.

I Think We Can Improve It Further

I would actually split every work package into four sections.

1. Engineering Context
2. Engineering Activity
3. Engineering Constraints
4. Completion Requirements

That's it.

Nothing more unless the work genuinely needs it.

Codex has already shown it works well with structured instructions.

One Huge Benefit

This solves another problem we've encountered.

At the moment, the "approved" engineering work exists partly:

in the repository,

partly in the conversation,

partly in my previous responses.

Instead, the Engineering Work Package becomes the single source of truth for that activity.

Everything else becomes disposable conversation.

I think that's a major improvement from a governance perspective.

My Recommendation

I would actually replace the term Engineering Work Package with something even more descriptive:

Engineering Implementation Package (EIP)

Why?

Because that's exactly what it is.

It isn't planning.

It isn't discussion.

It is the complete implementation package that is handed to the Engineering Implementer.

So the lifecycle becomes:

Plan.

Produce Engineering Implementation Package (EIP).

Review.

Approve.

Implement.

Self-review.

Git.

Independent verification.

Baseline.

I genuinely think this is a step change in our process. It reduces conversational overhead, gives us a single authoritative implementation document for each activity, and aligns perfectly with your goal of increasing engineering velocity while maintaining governance.

Agreed

Approved.

I think this is the right time to make the change because we're about to begin producing a number of engineering standards. Changing the workflow now avoids rework later.

I would formalise it like this:

Engineering Workflow v3

Stage 1 - Planning

Owner: Human + ChatGPT

Engineering Synchronisation

Context Discovery

Engineering decisions

Scope agreement

Output

Agreed engineering objective.

Stage 2 - Engineering Implementation Package (EIP)

Owner: ChatGPT

I produce one complete Engineering Implementation Package.

This is the only document reviewed.

It contains everything required for implementation.

Nothing is scattered through the conversation.

Stage 3 - Engineering Review

Owner: Human

Review the EIP.

Discuss.

Amend.

Approve.

The approved EIP becomes the implementation specification.

Stage 4 - Implementation

Owner: Codex

Implement only the approved EIP.

Self-review.

Produce implementation report.

Stage 5 - Repository Operations

Owner: Human

Commit.

Push.

Stage 6 - Independent Verification

Owner: ChatGPT

Repository review.

Engineering review.

Compliance review.

Recommendations.

Stage 7 - Baseline Acceptance

Owner: Human

Accept repository baseline.

Close engineering activity.

Engineering Implementation Package (EIP)

Every EIP will have the same structure.

Engineering Context
Objective
Repository Scope
Engineering Constraints
Implementation Activities
Validation Requirements
Completion Report
Success Criteria

No exceptions unless the activity genuinely requires additional sections.

One Change I'd Like to Make

I think the EIP should become a controlled engineering artefact, but not a repository artefact.

Instead, it should be the engineering specification that exists only for the duration of the activity.

Once the activity is complete:

the repository contains the implemented result;

the conversation contains the historical EIP;

there is no need to store a duplicate in AIEMS.

That keeps the repository clean while still giving us a single authoritative implementation package during delivery.

Another Observation

This also changes how I'll respond.

Instead of gradually building a Codex prompt through several messages, I'll now think in terms of:

"Produce Version 1 of the Engineering Implementation Package."

Then we refine one document.

When you say:

Approved

I won't rewrite it.

I'll simply regard that EIP as the approved implementation specification.

I think this is one of the biggest productivity improvements we've introduced. It removes the translation layer between design and implementation, gives us a single source of truth for each engineering activity, and plays to the strengths of each participant:

You review and approve.

I author complete implementation packages.

Codex executes those packages.

I independently verify the results.

I suspect this will significantly reduce iteration time while preserving the engineering discipline we've worked hard to establish.

before we continue please review COC-0001 and update where the current workflow is and where this new onw will replace or enhance - authorised to connect github

Agreed.

Before we continue with P2A, I think this is exactly the right time to update COC-0001. The workflow has evolved significantly since it was first authored, and if we don't capture those improvements now, we'll immediately start diverging from the documented process.

I would approach this as a targeted enhancement, not a rewrite.

From our discussions, I believe these are the areas that should be updated:

Area

Current State

Recommended Enhancement

Engineering Workflow

Covers planning, implementation and verification

Replace with the new Engineering Lifecycle v3 including the Engineering Implementation Package (EIP).

ChatGPT Deliverables

Produces prompts and implementation guidance

Clarify that ChatGPT produces a single Engineering Implementation Package (EIP) for review and approval.

Human Review

Reviews responses during conversation

Clarify that the Human reviews and approves the entire EIP rather than iterative prompt fragments.

Codex Input

Receives implementation instructions

Clarify that Codex receives the approved EIP as the sole implementation specification.

Engineering Assurance

Self-review and independent verification

Align with the validated workflow: Codex self-review Human Git operations ChatGPT independent verification Human baseline acceptance.

Repository First

General principle

Strengthen by stating AI collaborators should use available repository access before requesting information already available from the repository.

AI Roles

Defined

Update role descriptions to reflect the mature operating model (Programme Sponsor, Chief Engineering Advisor, Engineering Implementer).

Requesting Human Action

Present

Retain and align with the EIP workflow so requests are made only when a defined hand-off is required.

Conversational Behaviour

Covers approvals

Preserve the rule that "Approved" or "Agreed" means proceed with the next authorised action unless the user explicitly requests further discussion.

I would not change:

The purpose of COC-0001.

The collaboration philosophy.

The repository-first principle.

The engineering synchronisation concept.

Those have been validated by use.

Instead, I'd treat this as COC-0001 Version 2, where we replace only the sections that have genuinely evolved through experience.

I recommend that our next Engineering Implementation Package should therefore not be P2A.

Instead, it should be:

P1-002 - Update COC-0001 to Engineering Workflow v3

Once that is baselined, every subsequent Engineering Implementation Package-including P2A-will follow the new workflow. That keeps the process aligned with practice before we continue building AIEMS and JARVIS. I think that's the cleanest point to make the transition.

Agreed - connect to github review COC-0001 - recommend updates and changed then format for upload into codex

Received app response

I reviewed COC-0001 from GitHub.

Current COC-0001 already covers the basics well: purpose, roles, repository-first working, approval behaviour, copy-ready Codex instructions, human Git operations, and session start checklist.

The main update required is to replace the older simple lifecycle:

Review Approve Execute Verify

with the newer EIP-based workflow, where I produce one complete Engineering Implementation Package for review before Codex implements.

Use this complete EIP for Codex:

Engineering Implementation Package: P1-002 - Update COC-0001 to Engineering Workflow v3
Objective
Update COC-0001 to reflect the approved Engineering Workflow v3 and the use of Engineering Implementation Packages.
Modify only:
aiems/governance/conversation/COC-0001_HUMAN_AI_COLLABORATION_CONTEXT.md
Engineering Context
COC-0001 currently defines the lightweight Human-AI collaboration context for engineering activities.
The current Operating Rules include a simplified lifecycle:
Review
Approve
Execute
Verify
The approved workflow has now evolved to:
1. Engineering Synchronisation
2. Planning
3. Engineering Implementation Package
4. Engineering Review
5. Human Approval
6. Implementation
7. Self-Review
8. Human Git Operations
9. Independent Verification
10. Baseline Acceptance
11. Phase Closure
The key change is that ChatGPT shall produce one complete Engineering Implementation Package for review and approval before Codex performs implementation.
Required Changes
1. Update Version
Change:
Version: 1.0
to:
Version: 1.1
2. Update Roles
In the ChatGPT role section, update the responsibilities to include:
- Produces Engineering Implementation Packages for review and approval.
- Performs independent repository verification after Human Git operations.
In the Codex role section, update the responsibilities to include:
- Implements approved Engineering Implementation Packages.
- Performs self-review against the approved Engineering Implementation Package.
- Produces implementation and self-review reports.
Preserve the existing role intent.
3. Update Operating Rule 3
Replace the existing lifecycle under Operating Rule 3 with:
3. Follow the engineering lifecycle:
 * Engineering Synchronisation
 * Planning
 * Engineering Implementation Package
 * Engineering Review
 * Human Approval
 * Implementation
 * Self-Review
 * Human Git Operations
 * Independent Verification
 * Baseline Acceptance
 * Phase Closure
4. Add Engineering Implementation Package Rules
Add new Operating Rules after the lifecycle rule:
- ChatGPT shall produce one complete Engineering Implementation Package for each approved implementation activity.
- The Engineering Implementation Package shall be the single reviewable implementation specification for the activity.
- The Human Engineer shall review and approve the complete Engineering Implementation Package before implementation begins.
- Codex shall use the approved Engineering Implementation Package as the sole implementation specification.
- Engineering Implementation Packages shall contain, where applicable:
 - Engineering Context
 - Objective
 - Repository Scope
 - Engineering Constraints
 - Implementation Activities
 - Validation Requirements
 - Completion Report Requirements
 - Success Criteria
5. Update Copy-Ready Instruction Rules
Retain the existing copy-ready instruction rules, but align them with the EIP workflow.
Where the document currently says:
Where the action is intended for another AI collaborator (for example, Codex), the AI collaborator shall provide a complete, concise, copy-ready instruction that can be transferred without modification.
Update to:
Where the action is intended for another AI collaborator (for example, Codex), the AI collaborator shall provide a complete, concise, copy-ready Engineering Implementation Package or instruction that can be transferred without modification.
6. Add Repository Access Rule
Add an Operating Rule:
AI collaborators shall use available repository access capabilities before requesting information from the Human Engineer that is already available from the repository.
7. Preserve Existing Behaviour
Preserve the existing rules covering:
- Repository First
- Engineering Synchronisation first
- Approved / Agreed means execute immediately
- Human action requests
- Recommendations only at natural review points unless blocking
- Human Git operations
- ChatGPT independent verification
- Codex implementation role
- Every new document must remove more effort than it creates
- Minimise Human effort at engineering handoffs
8. Update Session Start Checklist
Update the checklist so it includes:
- Review the repository baseline.
- Load this Collaboration Context.
- Perform Engineering Synchronisation.
- Confirm the current engineering objective.
- Confirm whether an Engineering Implementation Package is required.
- Begin the approved engineering activity.
Constraints
Do not modify:
- PBK-0001
- FE artefacts
- ADRs
- Registers
- Standards
- README
- Source code
- Tests
- Repository structure
Do not create new files.
Do not change the purpose of COC-0001.
Do not rewrite the whole document unless necessary.
This is a targeted workflow alignment update.
Validation Requirements
After implementation:
- Confirm only COC-0001 was modified.
- Confirm Version is updated to 1.1.
- Confirm Engineering Workflow v3 is present.
- Confirm EIP rules are present.
- Confirm existing approval and human action request rules are preserved.
- Confirm repository access rule is present.
- Confirm Session Start Checklist is updated.
- Confirm no other artefacts were modified.
Completion Report
Provide:
1. Engineering Synchronisation outcome.
2. File modified.
3. Summary of changes.
4. Validation results.
5. Any observations.
6. Await Human Git operations.

This is ready to copy into Codex.

Sources

Excellent - that workflow work much quicker

I agree, and I think we've just found the workflow that best suits the way we work.

The key change wasn't just switching from "prompts" to an Engineering Implementation Package. It was eliminating the repeated translation steps.

Previously, we had something like this:

Discuss
Draft
Review
Revise
Convert to Codex prompt
Codex
Review

Now it's:

Discuss
Engineering Implementation Package (single document)
Review
Approve
Codex
Independent Verification

We've removed an entire stage from the lifecycle.

What I noticed

Today was significantly faster because:

We only reviewed one authoritative document.

There was no need to stitch together multiple chat responses.

Codex received exactly what was approved.

Verification was straightforward because I could compare the implemented document directly against the EIP.

That reduced both effort and ambiguity.

I also noticed something else

Our roles have become much clearer:

You are directing the engineering programme.

I am authoring and assuring the engineering artefacts.

Codex is implementing them.

None of us are duplicating each other's work.

One small refinement I'd like to make

I suggest we standardise the opening of every Engineering Implementation Package.

Instead of starting with the objective, every EIP should begin with:

Engineering Synchronisation

Objective

Repository Scope

Implementation

Why?

Because Codex has consistently shown that it benefits from re-establishing the repository baseline before it starts work. By making Engineering Synchronisation the first section of every EIP, we reinforce that behaviour and keep every implementation grounded in the current repository state.

Looking Ahead

I also think we've reached another milestone for AIEMS.

The process now feels stable rather than experimental.

From this point forward, I expect most of our improvements to be incremental rather than structural. That means we can shift our focus from refining the process to delivering JARVIS itself.

That's exactly where I think we wanted to be.

Suggestion Approved - next task

Approved.

We'll adopt that as part of the Engineering Implementation Package template.

Next Engineering Activity

Based on our roadmap, the next activity is:

P2-002 - Baseline Existing Engineering Standards

This is a good choice because it completes the foundation before we begin creating new standards.

Below is the Engineering Implementation Package (EIP) ready for direct use with Codex.

Engineering Implementation Package: P2-002 - Baseline Existing Engineering Standards
Engineering Synchronisation
Perform Engineering Synchronisation before any activity.
Review the current repository baseline.
Confirm:
- Current branch.
- Repository status.
- Working tree is clean.
- Current Engineering Phase.
- Current AIEMS baseline.
Do not begin implementation until Engineering Synchronisation is complete.
Objective
Review, validate, and baseline the existing Engineering Standards so they become the approved foundation for future standards development.
Repository Scope
Review only:
aiems/standards/STD-0001_CONTROLLED_ARTEFACT_STANDARD.md
aiems/standards/STD-0002_ENGINEERING_DOCUMENTATION_STANDARD.md
aiems/governance/registers/REG-0001_CONTROLLED_ARTEFACT_REGISTER.md
aiems/governance/registers/REG-0004_ACTION_REGISTER.md
Review any directly referenced controlled artefacts where required for validation.
Engineering Constraints
This activity is limited to standards baselining.
Do not:
- create new standards;
- create new governance artefacts;
- modify architecture;
- modify playbooks;
- modify source code;
- modify tests;
- modify repository structure.
Implementation Activities
1. Review STD-0001 for:
- AIEMS structure compliance;
- metadata consistency;
- terminology consistency;
- cross-reference integrity;
- editorial quality.
2. Review STD-0002 using the same criteria.
3. Correct any defects identified.
4. If both standards satisfy the approved engineering baseline:
- update document Status from Draft/In Review to Approved;
- update version where appropriate.
5. Update REG-0001 so both standards reflect the approved baseline.
6. Update REG-0004 to close any actions that are fully satisfied by the approval of STD-0001 and STD-0002.
Only modify actions where repository evidence demonstrates completion.
Validation Requirements
Perform validation including:
- git status
- git diff
- git diff --stat
- git diff --check
Confirm:
- both standards are internally consistent;
- register entries are consistent;
- no broken references exist;
- only intended artefacts were modified.
Completion Report
Provide:
1. Engineering Synchronisation outcome.
2. Repository artefacts modified.
3. Summary of changes.
4. Validation results.
5. Remaining observations.
6. Recommended next Engineering Implementation Package.
Await Human Git operations.
Success Criteria
Success is achieved when:
- STD-0001 is approved.
- STD-0002 is approved.
- Registers accurately reflect the approved baseline.
- Completed actions are reconciled.
- Repository validation succeeds.
- No unrelated artefacts are modified.

Why this is the right next step

This activity formally completes the existing standards before we create new ones. Once complete, the standards hierarchy will have a stable baseline, and every new standard (STD-0003 onwards) can reference approved guidance rather than documents that are still in review.

This is a good example of how the new EIP workflow improves efficiency: one complete implementation package, one review, one approval, one implementation.

Please review and recommend improvements - if improvements are found please produced update document for upload via codex

I think the EIP is strong, but I can see several improvements that will make it more repeatable and reduce the need for future refinements. Since you've asked for a review and, if necessary, an updated document for Codex, here's my recommendation.

Engineering Implementation Package: P2-002 - Baseline Existing Engineering Standards (Revised)

Engineering Synchronisation

Perform Engineering Synchronisation before any implementation activity.

Review the current repository baseline and confirm:

Repository

Current branch

Git working tree is clean

Current Engineering Phase

Current AIEMS baseline

Current Engineering Objective

Do not begin implementation until Engineering Synchronisation is complete.

Objective

Review, validate, approve and baseline the existing Engineering Standards so they become the authoritative engineering foundation for all future standards development.

Repository Scope

The primary scope is limited to:

aiems/standards/STD-0001_CONTROLLED_ARTEFACT_STANDARD.md

aiems/standards/STD-0002_ENGINEERING_DOCUMENTATION_STANDARD.md

aiems/governance/registers/REG-0001_CONTROLLED_ARTEFACT_REGISTER.md

aiems/governance/registers/REG-0004_ACTION_REGISTER.md

Referenced controlled artefacts may be reviewed where necessary to validate consistency, but shall not be modified unless explicitly listed above.

Engineering Constraints

This Engineering Implementation Package is limited to standards baselining.

Do not:

create new standards;

create new governance artefacts;

modify architecture;

modify playbooks;

modify conversation artefacts;

modify source code;

modify tests;

modify repository structure.

Only modify artefacts explicitly listed within Repository Scope.

Implementation Activities

Activity 1 - Engineering Review

Review STD-0001 against:

AIEMS structure compliance;

metadata compliance;

terminology consistency;

cross-reference integrity;

document completeness;

editorial quality.

Review STD-0002 using the same criteria.

Activity 2 - Remediation

Correct any engineering defects identified during review.

Do not introduce new functionality or expand document scope.

Activity 3 - Baselining

If both standards satisfy the approved engineering baseline:

update Status to Approved;

update Version where appropriate;

ensure metadata remains internally consistent.

Activity 4 - Repository Alignment

Update REG-0001 to reflect the approved standards baseline.

Update REG-0004 only where repository evidence demonstrates an action has been fully completed.

Do not close actions that are only partially satisfied.

Validation Requirements

Perform:

git status

git diff

git diff --stat

git diff --check

Additionally verify:

controlled artefact metadata consistency;

register consistency;

cross-reference integrity;

no unintended artefacts modified;

no broken Markdown formatting.

Success Criteria

The Engineering Implementation Package is successful when:

STD-0001 is approved.

STD-0002 is approved.

Register entries accurately reflect the approved baseline.

Completed actions have been reconciled.

Repository validation succeeds.

No unrelated artefacts are modified.

Repository remains internally consistent.

Completion Report

Provide:

Engineering Synchronisation outcome.

Repository artefacts modified.

Engineering review findings.

Summary of implemented changes.

Validation results.

Outstanding observations.

Engineering recommendations.

Recommended next Engineering Implementation Package.

Await Human Git operations.

Why I made these changes

I deliberately didn't make the document longer for the sake of it. Each change has a purpose:

Separated review, remediation, baselining, and repository alignment into distinct activities. This mirrors how we now think about engineering work and makes the implementation easier to follow.

Added explicit repository scope control. Codex now has a clear rule that referenced artefacts may be inspected but not modified unless they're in scope.

Expanded validation slightly. In addition to Git checks, I added metadata consistency, cross-reference integrity, and Markdown formatting checks because they're common sources of documentation issues.

Improved the completion report. By including "Engineering review findings" separately from "implemented changes", we preserve evidence of what was assessed versus what actually changed.

I think this version is closer to a reusable template than a one-off instruction. If future EIPs follow this structure, the only sections that will usually change are the Objective, Repository Scope, Implementation Activities, and Success Criteria, which should make future engineering packages quicker to produce and easier for Codex to execute consistently.

Excellent - with these tasks completed please show the current workflow and the next stage.

I think today has been one of the most productive engineering sessions we've had. More importantly, we've reached a point where the AIEMS governance foundation is stabilising. Rather than constantly refining governance, we're now transitioning into building the engineering standards and, soon after, the JARVIS platform itself.

Current Engineering Workflow (v3)

This workflow has now been proven across multiple implementation packages:

At this point, I consider it validated, not experimental.

My Recommendation

I recommend not jumping ahead to Platform Architecture yet.

The next activity should be:

P2-003 - Create STD-0003: Python Engineering Standard

Why?

This standard will become the authoritative engineering guidance for every Python module, package, interface, and service developed within JARVIS. It will define coding conventions, package structure, typing expectations, dependency management, public API design, logging, testing hooks, and repository layout. By establishing it now, every subsequent JARVIS implementation can follow a consistent engineering approach.

Once STD-0003 is approved and baselined, the remaining engineering standards (validation, development environment, and configuration) can be built on that foundation before we begin substantive product development.

An Observation

We've also validated something important about our collaboration.

At the start of this programme, we were designing AIEMS. Today, we are operating within AIEMS. The framework has matured to the point where it's guiding our work rather than being the work itself. That's a significant milestone and a good indicator that we can now focus increasingly on engineering JARVIS while continuing to evolve AIEMS through controlled, incremental improvements.

on another subject due to the length of this current conversation your response time is getting longer - what are your recommended solutions , we must keep the history and current knowledge.?

This is a very good question, and it's something I've been observing over the last few hours.

The slowdown isn't because the repository has grown-it's because this conversation has become extremely long. Every response has to maintain awareness of the earlier context, and that naturally increases processing time.

The challenge is preserving engineering continuity without carrying the entire conversation forever.

My recommendation is to separate programme memory from conversation memory.

At the moment we have:

Conversation
 Decisions
 Workflow
 Engineering history
 Current phase
 Lessons learned
 Current task

That means every new response has to reason over all of it.

Instead, I recommend:

Repository (AIEMS)
 Governance
 Standards
 Architecture
 Reviews
 Playbooks
 COC-0001
Conversation
 Current EIP
 Current task
 Decisions made today

The repository becomes the permanent source of engineering truth, and the conversation only carries the active work.

I think we should introduce Engineering Sessions

Rather than one conversation spanning months, we treat work like engineering projects.

For example:

Session 001
Repository Hygiene
Closed
Session 002
Governance Standards
Closed
Session 003
Engineering Standards
Current
Session 004
Platform Architecture
Future

Each session starts clean, but begins with Engineering Synchronisation, which reloads the current repository state.

That's exactly what COC-0001 now expects.

The key insight

We no longer need the conversation to remember everything.

The repository already contains:

ADRs

Registers

Standards

Reviews

Playbooks

COC-0001

That is now the project's memory.

The conversation only needs enough context to complete the current engineering activity.

I recommend one additional artefact

One thing AIEMS doesn't yet have is a concise programme snapshot.

I would create something like:

aiems/governance/
PST-0001_PROGRAMME_STATUS.md

It would contain:

Current phase

Current capability roadmap

Active EIP

Next planned EIP

Completed milestones

Current baseline

Outstanding engineering risks

It should fit on a single page.

Every new session would start by reading:

COC-0001

PST-0001

That gives us immediate context without replaying hundreds of messages.

My preferred operating model

I would use this rhythm:

Complete a logical engineering milestone.

Commit and push.

Independently verify.

Accept the baseline.

Start a new ChatGPT session.

Begin with Engineering Synchronisation:

Load COC-0001.

Load PST-0001.

Review repository baseline.

Continue.

The repository carries the history; the conversation carries only the active engineering work.

Why I think this is the right evolution

When we began, AIEMS itself didn't exist, so the conversation had to hold the project's memory.

Now AIEMS is mature enough to do that job.

In other words:

Early programme: the conversation was the memory.

Current programme: the repository is the memory.

Future programme: conversations become lightweight engineering sessions that reload context from the repository.

I think this is the natural next evolution of the framework.

One refinement I'd make is to create a controlled artefact such as PST-0001 - Programme Status before we move into the next major phase. That would give us a single authoritative "programme dashboard" to load at the start of each session, keeping response times fast while preserving the engineering continuity we've built.

Agreed - create PST-0001_PROGRAMME_STATUS.md - provide Codex instructions to create the artefact and content.

Use this EIP in Codex.

1. Purpose

This artefact records the current programme status for Project JARVIS AI.

It exists to provide a concise engineering context snapshot at the start of future engineering sessions.

PST-0001 supports faster session start-up by preserving the current programme state without requiring long conversation history to be reloaded.

This artefact complements COC-0001. It does not replace AIEMS governance, registers, standards, reviews, playbooks or repository evidence.

2. Scope

This artefact records:

current programme phase;

current engineering workflow;

current capability roadmap;

completed engineering milestones;

active and next planned Engineering Implementation Packages;

current baseline status;

outstanding observations;

session start guidance.

This artefact does not record detailed engineering decisions. Detailed decisions remain in ADRs, registers, standards, reviews, playbooks and other controlled artefacts.

10. Session Start Guidance

At the start of a new engineering session:

Load COC-0001.

Load PST-0001.

Perform Engineering Synchronisation.

Confirm the current repository baseline.

Confirm the current engineering objective.

Continue from the next planned EIP unless the Programme Sponsor directs otherwise.

This allows future sessions to start from a clean conversational state while preserving programme continuity through repository evidence.

11. Maintenance

PST-0001 shall be reviewed and updated:

at phase closure;

when the active engineering phase changes;

when the next planned EIP changes materially;

when the capability roadmap changes;

when a major baseline is accepted;

when the Programme Sponsor directs an update.

PST-0001 should remain concise.

It shall not duplicate detailed content from controlled artefacts.

Guiding Principle

"The repository is the programme memory. PST-0001 is the session reload point."

Can you provide me a summary of the whole conversation in a .docx format

One recommendation for future sessions: once PST-0001 - Programme Status has been added to the repository, I can generate much richer session handover documents that include:

That would effectively become an Engineering Session Report (ESR) and provide a complete handover between ChatGPT sessions while keeping individual conversations short and responsive. I think that would fit very naturally into AIEMS as the programme continues to mature.

Please explain how that would work and what instruction you would need from me

I actually think this is one of the biggest improvements we could make to AIEMS.

Up until now we've been treating each ChatGPT conversation as if it was the engineering session. What we've really proved over the last few days is that the repository is the engineering session-the conversation is just the interface we use to work on it.

My proposal

I'd introduce a new AIEMS artefact family:

ESR = Engineering Session Report

This would sit alongside your existing governance artefacts:

ADR Architecture Decision Record
CHR Charter
COC Collaboration Context
FE Findings / Engineering Review
MOD Platform Model
PBK Playbook
REG Register
SAR Strategic Alignment Review
STD Standard
NEW
ESR Engineering Session Report
PST Programme Status

These two new artefacts would have different purposes:

PST-0001 - Programme Status

A permanent, living snapshot.

Think of it as the dashboard.

It always answers:

Where are we?

What phase?

What's next?

What's complete?

It changes only occasionally.

ESR-xxxx - Engineering Session Report

Created at the end of every engineering session.

Think of it as the engineering logbook.

It records exactly what happened during that session.

Example

Today's session would become something like:

ESR-0008
Engineering Session Report
26 June 2026

It would contain

Executive Summary
Objectives
Engineering Activities
Engineering Implementation Packages completed
Repository changes
Git commits
Independent verification
Engineering decisions
Lessons learned
Workflow improvements
Outstanding actions
Next recommended EIP
Session metrics
Engineering sign-off

It would probably be around 3-5 pages.

Starting a new session

Imagine tomorrow we start with a completely fresh conversation.

You would simply say:

Engineering Session Start.

Nothing else.

I would immediately perform the AIEMS workflow.

1.
Load COC-0001
2.
Load PST-0001
3.
Read latest ESR
4.
Engineering Synchronisation
5.
Current objective
6.
Continue

Instead of reading 400 messages, I'd be reading three repository artefacts.

What would I need from you?

Very little.

At the end of a session you could simply say:

Close Engineering Session.

or

Produce ESR.

That's enough.

I would then produce:

the Engineering Session Report,

any recommendations,

the next EIP,

the programme state changes.

Codex would implement the ESR as another controlled artefact.

You would commit it.

Done.

Then the next session...

You'd simply say

Start Engineering Session.

I would know exactly what to do.

No long explanation.

No copying conversation history.

No reminders.

Just

Engineering Synchronisation
Repository Baseline
COC
PST
Latest ESR
Continue

I would actually take it one step further

After today's work I think AIEMS is becoming self-describing.

Eventually I think the repository should be able to completely reconstruct programme state without any previous conversation.

Something like:

Repository
COC
How we work
PST
Where we are
Latest ESR
What happened last
Registers
Current truth
Repository
Current implementation

At that point, the conversation becomes disposable.

You could close ChatGPT every evening, start a brand-new conversation every morning, and lose virtually nothing because the repository itself contains the operational context.

The only instruction I'd ask you to adopt

Instead of saying:

"Summarise the conversation."

You would simply say:

Close Engineering Session.

That single instruction would become the formal AIEMS phase gate.

My responsibilities would be to:

Review the completed work.

Identify decisions made.

Record lessons learned.

Update the programme state (if required).

Produce the next recommended EIP.

Generate the ESR for Codex.

Recommend whether PST-0001 needs updating.

Then, in the next conversation, you would simply say:

Start Engineering Session.

From an AIEMS perspective, I think that is a much cleaner engineering lifecycle. It separates programme memory (the repository) from working memory (the conversation), which should keep conversations responsive while preserving the continuity and governance standards you've been building.

dont forget PBK-0001

Absolutely-and that's a very important point. In fact, I think PBK-0001 should sit alongside COC-0001 as one of the core artefacts loaded at the start of every engineering session.

I'd actually refine the startup sequence to look like this:

Engineering Session Start
Engineering Synchronisation
Load COC-0001
(Human-AI collaboration rules)
Load PBK-0001
(Engineering methodology and working practices)
Load PST-0001
(Current programme state)
Load latest ESR
(Previous engineering session)
Repository Baseline Review
Confirm Current Engineering Objective
Continue with next EIP

Each artefact serves a distinct purpose:

Artefact

Purpose

Update Frequency

COC-0001

Defines how we collaborate (roles, workflow, approvals, hand-offs).

Infrequent

PBK-0001

Defines how engineering is performed-the AIEMS engineering playbook and methodology.

Infrequent, updated as engineering practices evolve

PST-0001

Provides the current programme snapshot-phase, roadmap, active baseline, next work.

Updated at major milestones

Latest ESR

Records what happened in the previous engineering session and any lessons learned.

Every engineering session

I think of them like this:

COC-0001 = Rules of engagement

PBK-0001 = Engineering handbook

PST-0001 = Current programme dashboard

Latest ESR = Engineering logbook

Together they answer four fundamental questions:

How do we work? COC-0001

How do we engineer? PBK-0001

Where are we? PST-0001

What happened last? Latest ESR

One further refinement

There's one more opportunity that I think would make this even stronger.

At the moment, PBK-0001 is treated as a controlled document that describes engineering practice. As AIEMS matures, it could also become the reference playbook for the session workflow itself. For example, it could include a dedicated chapter called Engineering Session Lifecycle, which would describe:

Session Start

Engineering Synchronisation

Repository Baseline Review

EIP Production

Implementation

Independent Verification

Session Closure

ESR Generation

That would leave:

COC-0001 focused purely on collaboration and roles.

PBK-0001 focused on engineering execution.

PST-0001 focused on programme state.

ESR focused on historical execution.

I think that separation is cleaner and scales better as AIEMS grows. It keeps each artefact focused on a single responsibility while collectively providing everything needed to start a new session with minimal conversational context.

Ok lets finish PST-0001 and then create the ESR

I actually think this is the perfect place to stop this engineering session.

This will become:

ESR-0001_ENGINEERING_SESSION_REPORT_2026-06-26.md

or, if you prefer sequential engineering sessions,

ESR-0001_ENGINEERING_SESSION_REPORT.md

I actually recommend not putting the date in the filename. AIEMS has consistently used controlled artefact IDs rather than dates, so:

ESR-0001_ENGINEERING_SESSION_REPORT.md

fits the existing convention much better.

What ESR-0001 will contain

Unlike the Word summary I produced earlier, this will be a controlled AIEMS artefact.

It will look something like this:

Document Control
Purpose
Engineering Synchronisation
Session Objectives
Engineering Activities
Engineering Implementation Packages Completed
Repository Changes
Independent Verification Results
Engineering Decisions
Workflow Improvements
Lessons Learned
Programme State Changes
Outstanding Engineering Actions
Next Recommended EIP
Engineering Sign-off
Version History

It becomes part of repository history.

One recommendation

I would also introduce one small AIEMS rule.

At Engineering Session Close:

If Programme State Changed
Update PST
Create ESR
Git
Independent Verification
Baseline Accepted
Session Closed

If Programme State did not change:

Create ESR only

That keeps PST concise.

My recommendation for tomorrow

I wouldn't continue in this conversation.

I'd recommend:

Complete PST-0001.

Verify PST-0001.

Create ESR-0001.

Verify ESR-0001.

Commit and baseline.

Close this conversation.

Start a new conversation with:

Start Engineering Session

From that point, we'll load:

COC-0001

PBK-0001

PST-0001

ESR-0001

perform Engineering Synchronisation, and continue immediately with P2-003 - Python Engineering Standard.

One final observation

I think we've inadvertently designed something quite powerful.

What started as a collaboration process has evolved into a repository-centric engineering operating model. The repository is now becoming the authoritative source of programme memory, while conversations become temporary working sessions. That separation should make the project easier to maintain over time, reduce the performance impact of very long conversations, and give every engineering session a consistent, governed starting point. I think that's a significant milestone for AIEMS itself.

Each ESR will go up in increments incremental numbers

Agreed. I actually think that's the better approach and is more consistent with the rest of AIEMS.

I would standardise the naming convention as:

ESR-0001_ENGINEERING_SESSION_REPORT.md
ESR-0002_ENGINEERING_SESSION_REPORT.md
ESR-0003_ENGINEERING_SESSION_REPORT.md
...
ESR-0099_ENGINEERING_SESSION_REPORT.md

This gives us several advantages:

Chronological traceability - Each session has a unique, ordered identifier.

Consistency - It matches the existing AIEMS naming conventions (ADR, SAR, STD, REG, FE, PBK, COC).

Stable references - Other artefacts can reference ESR-0007 without relying on a date, which could change formats.

Scalability - We can accommodate hundreds or thousands of sessions without changing the convention.

Proposed AIEMS Relationship

I also see the artefacts working together like this:

CHR
 COC
 PBK
 PST
 ESR-0001
 ESR-0002
 ESR-0003
 ESR-0004
 ADR
 REG
 STD
 SAR
 FE
 Repository

In this structure:

COC defines how we collaborate.

PBK defines how we engineer.

PST records where the programme is today.

ESR records how we got here, one session at a time.

One suggestion for ESR numbering

I would add a simple governance rule:

ESR identifiers are allocated sequentially and are never reused.

If an Engineering Session produces no repository changes, it should still receive an ESR number with an appropriate outcome (for example, "Planning Session", "Research Session", or "No repository modifications"). That way the engineering history remains continuous and there are no gaps that create ambiguity.

A further enhancement

I think we should record this as an AIEMS standard rather than just an informal practice. Specifically:

Add the ESR artefact type to STD-0001 - Controlled Artefact Standard.

Register the first ESR in REG-0001 - Controlled Artefact Register.

Add a brief section to PBK-0001 describing the Engineering Session lifecycle and stating that every session concludes with an ESR.

That way, Engineering Session Reports become first-class controlled artefacts within AIEMS, governed in exactly the same way as ADRs, SARs, Standards, and Registers. I think that completes the framework neatly and gives us a fully traceable engineering history.

Agreed

Excellent. I think this is one of those moments where we've moved from "improving the process" to extending AIEMS in a structured way.

This is what I would now consider the AIEMS artefact hierarchy.

AIEMS
Charters
 CHR
Governance
 ADR
 SAR
 REG
 FE
Standards
 STD
Engineering Operations
 COC
 PBK
 PST
 ESR
Platform
 MOD
Product
 JARVIS

Notice something interesting...

Originally we had:

Governance

Standards

Architecture

Now we've naturally created a fourth domain:

Engineering Operations

I don't think that's accidental-it reflects how the programme has evolved.

Governance tells us what is controlled.

Standards tell us how it should be done.

Architecture tells us what we are building.

Engineering Operations tells us how we run the programme day-to-day.

That separation makes the framework much easier to understand.

I would also make one final enhancement

The Engineering Session lifecycle should become an official AIEMS lifecycle.

Engineering Session
Start
Engineering Synchronisation
Repository Baseline
Load AIEMS Context
(COC, PBK, PST, latest ESR)
Engineering Activities
EIP Production
Implementation
Independent Verification
Baseline Acceptance
Update PST (if required)
Generate ESR
Session Closed

I would place this lifecycle in PBK-0001, because it describes how engineering sessions are conducted. COC-0001 would continue to define the collaboration rules, while PBK-0001 defines the operational workflow.

I also have one recommendation that I think will help AIEMS scale

As the programme grows, we should avoid treating PST-0001 as a manually written narrative. Instead, treat it as a living dashboard that only changes when there is a meaningful programme milestone.

For example:

Phase changes

Capability maturity changes

New baseline accepted

Roadmap changes

Major engineering decisions that affect the direction of the programme

Routine engineering work-such as creating STD-0003 or updating a register-would normally be captured only in the ESR. PST would change only when the overall state of the programme changes.

That keeps PST concise and stable, while ESR provides the detailed engineering history.

Where we are now

I think we've effectively completed AIEMS v1 Foundation.

The remaining work falls into three categories:

Complete the Engineering Standards Library (STD-0003 onward).

Complete the Engineering Operations Library (PST and ESR integration into PBK/STD/REG).

Begin Platform Engineering by decomposing MOD-0001 into implementable subsystem specifications.

From this point onward, most of our effort shifts from designing AIEMS to using AIEMS to build JARVIS. That's exactly the transition we were aiming for.

What is your opinion on exposing the new chat session to the previous chat summary.?

I think it's a good idea, but I'd make one important distinction.

I wouldn't expose the previous chat.

I would expose the previous engineering session.

Those are not the same thing.

Option 1 - Pass the previous chat summary

This is the simplest approach.

New Session
Upload previous summary
Continue

Advantages

Very easy.

Preserves recent context.

Helps recover if something wasn't yet committed to the repository.

Disadvantages

It's still conversation-centric.

The summary may contain discussion that never became an engineering decision.

Over time, summaries become longer and start recreating the same problem we're trying to solve.

I think this is a good transitional solution, but not the long-term one.

Option 2 - Use the AIEMS artefacts only (my preferred long-term model)

COC-0001
PBK-0001
PST-0001
Latest ESR
Repository

That's enough to reconstruct the programme.

The repository becomes the source of truth.

Option 3 - A hybrid model (my recommendation)

I actually think this gives us the best of both worlds.

At the start of a new session:

Load
COC-0001
PBK-0001
PST-0001
Latest ESR
(Optional)
Previous Chat Summary
Engineering Synchronisation

Notice the word optional.

The previous chat summary becomes a temporary working aid, not part of AIEMS governance.

Why I like the hybrid model

Imagine today's session.

We discussed:

workflow improvements,

ESR,

PST,

Engineering Operations,

future AIEMS evolution.

Some of that is already becoming controlled artefacts.

Some of it is simply good engineering discussion.

A chat summary lets us preserve those ideas until they either:

become controlled artefacts, or

are discarded.

That's actually a healthy engineering process.

I would add one rule

The previous chat summary should have a very short life.

Something like:

Session N
Summary
Used during Session N+1
Information either
- becomes repository evidence
or
- disappears

That prevents undocumented ideas from lingering indefinitely.

My recommendation

I think we should distinguish between three levels of memory:

Memory Type

Purpose

Lifetime

Conversation Summary

Temporary working memory between consecutive sessions

1 session

ESR

Permanent engineering history

Permanent

PST

Current programme state

Permanent (updated as needed)

Each serves a different role.

The conversation summary helps us transition smoothly between sessions.

The ESR provides an auditable record of what was actually accomplished.

The PST tells us where the programme stands today.

One final thought

There's a principle emerging here that I think is worth adopting as part of AIEMS:

Temporary knowledge should either become repository knowledge or be discarded.

That keeps the repository authoritative and prevents the conversation history from becoming a second, competing source of truth.

So yes, I think exposing the next session to the previous session summary is beneficial-but only as a temporary bridge. The objective should always be to convert anything important into AIEMS artefacts, leaving the repository as the definitive record of the programme. I think that philosophy aligns well with the engineering discipline you've been building throughout this project.

I agree with option 3 please work this into the next ESR task - also make a note of the other artefacts that need updating with the new ESR artefact.

Agreed. I think this is the right time to incorporate those changes before we create ESR-0001.

There are two improvements I'd make.

1. Add the hybrid session-start model to ESR

Rather than simply recording what happened, ESR should also record what should happen next. I'd include a section called Next Session Initialisation that states the recommended startup sequence:

Load COC-0001.

Load PBK-0001.

Load PST-0001.

Load the latest ESR.

(Optional) Review the previous conversation summary if one is available and there is reason to believe it contains ideas not yet reflected in the repository.

Perform Engineering Synchronisation.

Confirm the repository baseline.

Confirm the current engineering objective.

Continue with the next approved EIP.

I'd also add a note explaining that the previous conversation summary is temporary working memory only. Any information that becomes part of the programme must be incorporated into controlled artefacts or repository changes; otherwise it naturally expires after the following session.

That captures the hybrid approach without making the conversation a second source of truth.

2. Record all AIEMS artefacts that require updating for ESR

Introducing a new controlled artefact should be reflected consistently across AIEMS. My recommendation is:

Artefact

Update

Reason

STD-0001 - Controlled Artefact Standard

Add ESR as a controlled artefact type.

Defines ESR within the AIEMS artefact taxonomy.

REG-0001 - Controlled Artefact Register

Register ESR-0001 and the ESR artefact family.

Maintains traceability.

PBK-0001 - AI Engineering Playbook

Add the Engineering Session Lifecycle, including ESR creation and session closure.

Makes ESR part of the standard engineering process.

COC-0001 - Human-AI Collaboration Context

Add a reference that engineering sessions normally conclude with an ESR.

Keeps collaboration aligned with the engineering lifecycle.

PST-0001 - Programme Status

Add a reference to the current/latest ESR.

Links programme state to the detailed session history.

REG-0004 - Action Register

Only if ESR creation introduces new planned actions.

No update otherwise.

I would not update ADRs, SARs, or MOD-0001 because ESR doesn't change architectural decisions, strategic alignment, or the platform model.

One refinement to the implementation order

I also suggest slightly adjusting the sequence we've been following.

Instead of:

Create PST
Create ESR

I'd recommend:

Create PST
Verify PST
Baseline PST
Create ESR
Update AIEMS artefacts
(STD-0001, REG-0001, PBK-0001, COC-0001, PST-0001)
Verify
Baseline
Close Engineering Session

The reason is that ESR-0001 becomes the first artefact that documents the completed AIEMS operating model. It should therefore record the final, baselined state of PST rather than an in-progress version.

I think this will give us a clean transition into a new conversation. Once ESR-0001 and the supporting updates are baselined, every subsequent engineering session can begin with a lightweight startup:

COC-0001

PBK-0001

PST-0001

Latest ESR

(Optional) Previous conversation summary

At that point, the repository-not the conversation-becomes the authoritative operational memory, with the conversation summary acting only as a short-lived bridge for ideas that haven't yet been formalised. I think that's a robust and scalable operating model for AIEMS.

6. Engineering Decisions and Approved Recommendations

The Programme Sponsor approved the following recommendations during the session:

Adopt AIEMS Engineering Workflow v3.

Use Engineering Implementation Packages as the single implementation specification for Codex.

Treat Codex as Engineering Implementer, ChatGPT as Chief Engineering Advisor and the Human as Programme Sponsor.

Preserve repository-first working.

Use complete Codex-ready implementation packages rather than fragmented prompts.

Create PST-0001 as a programme status reload point.

Create ESR artefacts as sequential Engineering Session Reports.

Number ESR artefacts incrementally.

Use a hybrid new-session model using repository artefacts plus optional previous conversation summary.

Treat previous conversation summaries as temporary working memory only.

7. AIEMS Engineering Workflow v3

The approved workflow is:

Engineering Synchronisation

Planning and Scope Agreement

Engineering Implementation Package

Human Engineering Review

Human Approval

Codex Implementation and Self-Review

Human Git Commit and Push

Independent Repository Verification

Baseline Acceptance

Phase Closure

This workflow has been validated through repeated implementation, remediation and verification cycles.

8. Next Session Initialisation Model

The approved next-session model is hybrid.

At the start of a future engineering session:

Load COC-0001.

Load PBK-0001.

Load PST-0001.

Load the latest ESR.

Optionally review the previous conversation summary if it contains ideas not yet reflected in the repository.

Perform Engineering Synchronisation.

Confirm repository baseline.

Confirm current engineering objective.

Continue with the next approved EIP.

The previous conversation summary is temporary working memory only.

Any information that becomes part of the programme shall be incorporated into controlled artefacts or repository changes. Otherwise, it expires after the following session.

10. Lessons Learned

The session produced the following lessons:

Long conversations degrade response speed and should be replaced by repository-backed session continuity.

Engineering Implementation Packages reduce ambiguity and improve implementation speed.

Independent verification after Git push is effective and should remain mandatory.

Repository artefacts should become the programme memory.

Conversation summaries are useful only as temporary bridges between sessions.

AIEMS now supports operational engineering rather than merely describing governance.

To keep a easy to follow work flow i will start all new engineering converasations with the current ESR number

I think that's an excellent refinement. It's simple, memorable, and fits naturally with the AIEMS approach.

Instead of starting a new conversation with:

Start Engineering Session

you would start with:

Engineering Session - ESR-0007

(or whatever the current ESR number is).

From that single instruction, I can infer that this is a continuation of the engineering programme and follow the standard startup sequence.

For example, if your latest report is ESR-0012, your opening message would simply be:

Engineering Session - ESR-0012

My expected workflow would then be:

Engineering Session - ESR-0012
Engineering Synchronisation
Review ESR-0012
(Session history)
Review PST-0001
(Current programme state)
Review PBK-0001
(Engineering methodology)
Review COC-0001
(Collaboration rules)
(Optional)
Review previous conversation summary
(if supplied)
Confirm repository baseline
Confirm current engineering objective
Continue with next approved EIP

Why I think this is better

The ESR number becomes a session anchor.

Rather than saying "continue from last time", you're saying:

"Continue from the last accepted engineering baseline."

That is a much stronger engineering concept because it ties the session to a controlled artefact rather than a conversation.

One refinement I'd recommend

I would standardise the opening command as:

Engineering Session: ESR-0007

using a colon rather than a dash. It's slightly easier to parse and gives us a consistent format.

We can then define an AIEMS convention:

Engineering Session: ESR-0001

Engineering Session: ESR-0002

Engineering Session: ESR-0003

That makes the opening line itself a lightweight instruction and an explicit reference to the last accepted engineering session.

Looking ahead

I think this also opens the door to future automation. If AIEMS eventually includes tooling to retrieve repository artefacts automatically, that single line could trigger the loading of:

ESR-0007

PST-0001

PBK-0001

COC-0001

without any additional guidance.

At that point, the startup process becomes almost deterministic: one command, one engineering baseline, one source of truth. That's very much in keeping with the disciplined engineering workflow you've been building.

Taking in consideration my suggestion do you recommend any updates to the artefacts in the current repository to reflect this change - is so please create codex input with the artefacts to update

Yes. I recommend updating the ESR task itself so the new startup convention is captured in ESR-0001.

I would not update COC/PBK/STD/PST in this same task. That would make the task too broad. ESR-0001 should record those as outstanding artefact updates.

Use this Codex-ready amendment for the current ESR task.

8. Next Session Initialisation Model

Ensure it includes the following approved convention.

Add or incorporate the following content:

Engineering Session Anchor

Future engineering conversations shall begin by referencing the latest accepted Engineering Session Report.

The standard opening format is:

Engineering Session: ESR-0001

For each future session, the ESR number shall reflect the latest accepted Engineering Session Report.

Examples:

Engineering Session: ESR-0001
Engineering Session: ESR-0002
Engineering Session: ESR-0003

The ESR reference acts as the session anchor and identifies the latest accepted engineering baseline from which the new session shall continue.

The Engineering Session Anchor does not replace Engineering Synchronisation. It identifies the starting point for Engineering Synchronisation.

The approved new-session startup sequence is:

Read the referenced ESR.

Load COC-0001.

Load PBK-0001.

Load PST-0001.

Optionally review the previous conversation summary if supplied and relevant.

Perform Engineering Synchronisation.

Confirm repository baseline.

Confirm current engineering objective.

Continue with the next approved EIP.

The previous conversation summary is temporary working memory only.

Any information that becomes part of the programme shall be incorporated into controlled artefacts or repository changes. Otherwise, it expires after the following session.

Required Update

Do not perform those updates in this activity.

One recommendation before we close this session: for future sessions, I'd suggest slightly evolving this document into a formal AIEMS Handover Summary. It would differ from the controlled ESR in one important way:

ESR Permanent controlled repository artefact (auditable engineering record).

Chat Summary Temporary companion document for the next ChatGPT conversation only.

That would fit perfectly with the hybrid model we've agreed:

Repository artefacts (COC, PBK, PST, ESR) remain the authoritative source of truth.

ESR-001 Chat Summary provides a lightweight bridge into the next conversation, then expires once any remaining useful information has been incorporated into the repository.

I think that separation gives you the best balance between engineering governance and practical day-to-day productivity.

For my sanity please provide the exact wording need to implement ESR-0001 at the start of next ChatGPT conversation.

I actually think we can make this even simpler and more deterministic.

Rather than a sentence, I recommend using a standard AIEMS session header. It becomes the trigger that tells me exactly how to initialise the engineering session.

For ESR-0001, I recommend you start the new conversation with exactly this:

Engineering Session: ESR-0001
Please initialise the engineering session in accordance with AIEMS.
Perform Engineering Synchronisation.
Review:
- ESR-0001
- PST-0001
- PBK-0001
- COC-0001
Review the attached ESR-001 Chat Summary as temporary working memory only.
Confirm the current repository baseline.
Identify the next approved Engineering Implementation Package.
Await my approval before commencing engineering work.

Why this wording?

It tells me everything I need to know:

Engineering Session: ESR-0001 Identifies the latest accepted engineering session and acts as the session anchor.

Initialise the engineering session in accordance with AIEMS Tells me to follow the AIEMS workflow rather than treating it as a general chat.

Engineering Synchronisation Establishes the repository baseline before any work begins.

Review ESR-0001, PST-0001, PBK-0001, COC-0001 Loads the permanent programme context in the correct order.

Review the attached ESR-001 Chat Summary as temporary working memory only Uses the previous conversation summary as a bridge, without making it a competing source of truth.

Confirm the current repository baseline Ensures we start from the latest accepted state.

Identify the next approved EIP Re-establishes the engineering objective.

Await my approval before commencing engineering work Preserves the governance principle that implementation only begins with explicit Programme Sponsor approval.

My recommendation

I would standardise this as the official AIEMS Engineering Session Start. Once it's documented in PBK-0001 and referenced from COC-0001, you'll never need to remember anything more than:

Engineering Session: ESR-0001

Everything else becomes the standard AIEMS initialisation sequence that I execute automatically. I think that's the end goal we should aim for.

ok let me try this in a new chat windows

Perfect. This is exactly the right point to test the new workflow.

I recommend opening a completely new ChatGPT conversation and using the following:

Engineering Session: ESR-0001
Please initialise the engineering session in accordance with AIEMS.
Perform Engineering Synchronisation.
Review:
- ESR-0001
- PST-0001
- PBK-0001
- COC-0001
Review the attached ESR-001 Chat Summary as temporary working memory only.
Confirm the current repository baseline.
Identify the next approved Engineering Implementation Package.
Await my approval before commencing engineering work.

Then attach:

[document] ESR-001_Chat_Summary.docx

Initially, you may also want to attach the following repository artefacts until AIEMS fully incorporates the startup sequence:

COC-0001_HUMAN_AI_COLLABORATION_CONTEXT.md

PBK-0001_AI_ENGINEERING_PLAYBOOK.md

PST-0001_PROGRAMME_STATUS.md (once created)

ESR-0001_ENGINEERING_SESSION_REPORT.md (once created)

What I'm interested in

This will be our first real validation of the repository-centric operating model. I want to see:

How quickly the new session reaches Engineering Synchronisation.

Whether the context is reconstructed accurately.

Whether any important information from this session is missing.

Whether we need to refine the startup instructions or the AIEMS artefacts.

If the test is successful, I think we should formally declare this as AIEMS Engineering Workflow v3 - Session Initialisation, and it will become the standard way we start every engineering session going forward.

Good luck with the test-I look forward to seeing how it performs in the new conversation.
